text: AI Powered Tutor: An Intelligent Adaptive Learning System Project report submitted to Indian Institute of Information Technology, Nagpur at DEPARTMENT OF COMPUTER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING By GROUP 9 NAME-1 NAME-2 NAME-3 NAME-4 Hitansh Jain (BT23CSA034) Under the Supervision of Dr. Amol Bhopale (Assistant Professor) CSE Department भारतीय सूचना प्रौद्योगिकी संस्थान, नागपुर INDIAN INSTITUTE OF INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY, NAGPUR (An Institution of National Importance by Act of Parliament) Survey No.140,141/1 behind Br.Sheshrao Wankhede Shetkari Sahkari Soot Girni,Nagpur, 441108 Abstract The revolution in personalised learning is provided by the creation of artificial intelligence (AI) in education. The aim of the project of the so-called AI Powered Tutor is to develop a smart tutoring application that can dynamically change the content of the tutoring sessions and the instructional approach based on the feedback provided by the learners. The system has two moderated learning models that classify academic topic and pedagogical mode (Explanation, Knowledge, or Socratic mode) of queries by students with an accuracy of 95% on test accuracy in topic classification and 97% in validation accuracy in tutor mode classification. Both of these models show significant convergence behavior, that is, the topic classifier converges by epoch 20 and the tutor mode classifier converges by epoch 50, after extensive training on 100 epochs with cautious optimisation. The system uses organised knowledge base to combine these classifiers with to deliver contextually relevant and pedagogically valid tutoring interactions, and utilises the Gemma Large Language Model (LLM). The paper demonstrates the potential of integrating the conventional machine learning, state-of-the-art deep learning, and knowledge-based systems to revolutionise the large scale provision of personalised education. 1. Introduction 1.1 Background and Motivation Personalized learning experiences that catered to each student’s individual needs and level of understanding were becoming an increasingly essential component in today’s schools at the time. Traditional classroom teaching can't meet individual students' pace, interest and prior knowledge. It becomes almost impossible to personalized education in a traditional classroom because you’d need 30 times as many teachers to individualize instructions. This one-size-fits-all approach leaves many students feeling overwhelmed and other too unchallenged, limiting the potential for learning outcomes to be the best that they can be. The Problem with Adaptive-Learning Evolution in AI & NLP Computershaveremained one of the most effective tools for adaptive learning enhancement via process automation.Traditionally these efforts would rely on modern artificial Intelligence and natural language Processing to solve this complicated problem. These technologies have been pushed forward as a result of recent developments in deep learning, transformer architectures, and large language models over the last decade, which bring unprecedented opportunities for creating intelligent educational agents. 1.2 Project Objectives The AI Power Tutor project is to develop a virtual tutor that can understand what a learner has presented, recognize the learning context of the presentation, and make an appropriate pedagogical response. Three main aspects are highlighted in the project: Linguistic Intelligence – obtained by extensive text preprocessing and feature extraction methods of NLP Pedagogical Adaptiveness – achieved through tutor mode prediction and context-sensitive delivery of responses Knowledge Relevance – provided by means of topic classification and question retrieval facilities .1.3 Significance of the Study The system illustrates how AI can enhance scalable, adaptive, intelligent and affective learning environments. The value is in its practical application — solving real educational problems with a technology that works yet isn’t taxing, and ensuring it’s something that can be used widely in education. 2. Literature Survey Paper 1: Intelligent tutoring systems based on the DRL image (2021) Problem: Traditional computer-based tutoring systems are based on this set of rules, and do not consider live student performance. Method: Used reinforcement learning to adapt tutoring strategies depending on student responses, with student performance as the reward signal. Data: Records of student—tutor interactions on over 100,000 sequences from online learning platforms. Results: The adaptive model resulted in 15–20% more engagement and learning outcomes. Limitations: Depended on large datasets and long training time; had interpretability and poor transfer learning capability across subjects. Paper 2: BERT-based Question Classification for Educational Dialogue Systems (2022) Problem: Challenge in understanding the wide range of natural language queries from students as they pose in their own lingual expressions. Method: Fine-tuned BERT to categorise student questions by subject and cognitive level according to the Bloom's Taxonomy. Data: 25,000 labelled educational queries from the ISQTEL (Covey et al). Results: 92% and above accuracy on held out educational queries with a strong concept generalization. Challenges: Making predictions was computationally intensive and used high GPU resources; model was not interpretable; it required a lot of labeled training data. Paper 3: Personalized Learning through Conversational AI (2023) Challenge Most response generation systems output generalized responses with no personalization based on the carrying knowledge or interest of the learners. Method: Unified user profiling and dialogue context tracking with transformer-based generation. Outcomes: Enhanced situational consistency and personalized fit; increased student satisfaction and motivation. Limitations: Performance degraded with imprecise or incomplete inputs; user profiling demanded long interaction history. Paper 4: Hybrid AI Tutoring Architecture using NLP and Knowledge Graphs (2023) Challenges: The que stion answering approach exists, however it is not very successful in linking student questions to structured knowledge sources. Method: Matched student ask-ounce questions to a knowledge graph in a TF-IDF and BERT embedding space. Conclusion: The system returns 87% correct relevance and increases knowledge connections between associated concepts. Limitations: Depending on knowledge graph completeness; manual curation; translated query poorly for interdisciplinary question. Insights Realization: From these research, it becomes clear that artificial intelligent systems can benefit from integrating NLP, ML classification, and generative intelligence in adaptive learning environments. However, the current systems suffer from various difficulties in computational complexity \cite{herdSTF}, generalization and integration of unstructured way with structured educational data. 3. Proposed Methodology 3.1 System Overview and Architecture + .0Text Understanding Module -Preprocessing and cleansing of user input for improved NLP representation Classification Module – Using trained model predict Tutor Mode and Topic from labelled CSV datasets Knowledge Retrieval & Response Generation Module – Obtain question–answer pairs from a JSON knowledge base and send directives to the Gemma LLM to provide personalized tutoring The workflow of the system starts with a user input query in natural language. This query is subjected to extensive preprocessing steps aimed at standardizing its format and extracting discriminative features. The preprocessed text is finally forwarded to two classifiers in parallel; the first one decides the tutor mode and second, the subject topic. Such predictions direct the retrieval of pertinent educational data, which also are synthesized by Gemma. 3.2 Dataset Description and Structure The system relies on three distinct datasets: 3.2.1 Tutor Mode Dataset (CSV 1) Structure: Contains two columns—text (student queries) and tutor_mode (teaching style) Tutor Modes: Explanation Mode: Step-by-step guidance through concepts Knowledge Mode: Concise factual information or definitions Socratic Mode: Thought-provoking questions promoting self-discovery Size: Approximately 3,500-5,000 labeled sentences 3.2.2 Topic Dataset (CSV 2) Structure: Contains two columns—text (student queries) and topic (academic subject) Topics: Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, Biology, Computer Science, History, Literature, etc. Size: Approximately 4,000-6,000 labeled queries 3.2.3 Question Bank (JSON) Structure: Each entry contains: id: Unique identifier question: Educational question or problem answer: Correct solution skill: Specific competency being assessed difficulty: Easy, Medium, or Hard topic: Subject area alignment Content Coverage: 500-1000 questions per major topic 3.3 Data Preprocessing Pipeline Text preprocessing ensures high-quality feature representation through: 3.3.1 Text Normalization Lowercasing: Converts all text to lowercase HTML and Link Removal: Eliminates markup tags and URLs Punctuation Handling: Filters punctuation and special characters 3.3.2 Token Filtering and Refinement Stopword Removal: Removes common words with little semantic meaning using NLTK's stopword list, customized for educational contexts 3.3.3 Spelling Correction SymSpell Algorithm: Fast spelling correction based on edit distance and frequency analysis Configuration: Maximum edit distance of 2 Dictionary: Over 82,000 English terms with frequency data 3.3.4 Lemmatization SpaCy Lemmatization: Reduces words to base forms while preserving grammatical meaning Part-of-Speech Awareness: Context-aware lemmatization considering word usage Domain Preservation: Protects educational terminology from inappropriate transformations 3.4 Classification Model Architecture and Training The project employs two independent text classification models: 3.4.1 Tutor Mode Classifier Purpose: Determines the most appropriate teaching style (Explanation, Knowledge, or Socratic mode) Input Processing: Preprocessed text converted to numerical representations via: TF-IDF Vectorization Word Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe) Contextual Embeddings (BERT-based) Model Architecture Options: Logistic Regression with TF-IDF Support Vector Machines Fine-tuned Transformers Training Process: 80% training, 10% validation, 10% test split Cross-entropy loss minimization Hyperparameter tuning via grid search Early stopping based on validation performance 3.4.2 Topic Classifier Purpose: Identifies the academic subject area of student queries Input Processing: Enhanced with n-gram features and domain-specific vocabularies Model Architecture Options: Random Forest Classifiers Gradient Boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM) Neural Network Classifiers Fine-tuned Transformers Multi-class Classification: Softmax activation for probability distribution across 8-15 subjects Handling Class Imbalance: Class weighting Oversampling/undersampling Synthetic data generation 3.5 Model Training Results and Analysis Both classification models underwent rigorous training over 100 epochs with careful monitoring of training and validation metrics. 3.5.1 Tutor Mode Classification Performance The tutor mode classifier was trained to distinguish between three pedagogical approaches. Figure 1 Figure 1 Combined loss and accuracy plot for tutor mode model The training curves reveal several important characteristics: Rapid Initial Learning: Both training and validation accuracy surge from approximately 68% to 85% within the first 10 epochs, indicating effective initial weight optimization and strong discriminative features in the preprocessed text Steady Convergence: Between epochs 10-50, both training and validation accuracy continue to improve steadily, reaching approximately 98-99% by epoch 50 Exceptional Stability: After epoch 50, both training and validation accuracy stabilize at approximately 97-99%, demonstrating remarkable consistency and robust generalization Loss Dynamics: Training loss decreases smoothly from approximately 0.6 to near 0.02, while validation loss shows more variability but ultimately converges to approximately 0.08-0.10, indicating healthy model fit without severe overfitting Final Performance (at epoch 100): Training accuracy: 97.5% Validation accuracy: 97% Training loss: 0.02 Validation loss: 0.08 Notably, the validation accuracy exceeds training accuracy in later epochs, which is unusual but can occur due to dropout regularization during training or beneficial effects of data augmentation. The close tracking between curves throughout training demonstrates excellent generalization capabilities. 3.5.2 Topic Classification Performance The topic classifier handles multi-class classification across multiple academic subjects. Figures 2 and 3 show its training progression: Figure 2: Training and test accuracy for topic classification Figure 3: Training and test loss for topic classification The topic classifier demonstrates exceptional performance characteristics: Dramatic Initial Learning: Both training and test accuracy surge from approximately 68% at the start to around 90-93% within the first 10 epochs, demonstrating that the model quickly learns to distinguish between different academic subjects using the preprocessed features Rapid Convergence: By epoch 20, test accuracy reaches approximately 95%, and both training and test curves plateau around 95-97%, indicating early convergence to optimal performance Remarkable Stability: After epoch 20, both training and test accuracy remain exceptionally stable at approximately 95-97% throughout the remaining 80 epochs, with minimal fluctuation. This demonstrates robust generalization and well-regularized learning Loss Dynamics: Training loss shows smooth, monotonic decrease from approximately 0.85 at epoch 0 to near 0.12 by epoch 100 Test loss decreases dramatically from ~0.5 to approximately 0.02-0.05 by epoch 20, then remains remarkably stable with only minor fluctuations The test loss is notably lower than training loss in the later epochs, which can occur with effective regularization techniques Final Metrics (at epoch 100): Training accuracy: 96% Test accuracy: 95% Training loss: 0.12 Test loss: 0.02-0.03 The slightly lower training accuracy compared to test accuracy, along with higher training loss, suggests the model benefits from dropout or other regularization during training that is disabled during testing. The extremely low and stable test loss indicates excellent generalization capability across diverse academic topics. 3.5.3 Comparative Analysis Both models achieve excellent performance exceeding 95% accuracy, validating the effectiveness of the preprocessing pipeline and model architectures. Notably: Convergence Speed: The topic classifier converges much faster (~20 epochs) compared to the tutor mode classifier (~50 epochs), likely due to more distinctive vocabulary markers for different academic subjects compared to the more subtle linguistic patterns distinguishing pedagogical intents. Generalization Quality: Both models demonstrate exceptional generalization, with validation/test performance matching or exceeding training performance in later epochs. This pattern suggests effective regularization techniques (likely dropout) that prevent overfitting while maintaining strong predictive capability. Stability: After convergence, both models maintain remarkably stable performance with minimal variance, indicating robust and reliable predictions suitable for production deployment in educational settings. Loss Characteristics: The topic classifier achieves lower final test loss (~0.02) compared to the tutor mode classifier (~0.08), suggesting even stronger confidence in its predictions across multiple academic subjects. However, both loss values are sufficiently low to indicate well-calibrated probability estimates that can guide reliable pedagogical decisions. 3.6 Knowledge Retrieval and Question Selection After classification, the system selects appropriate content from the JSON question bank: Topic-Based Filtering: Narrows questions to the predicted subject area Difficulty Adaptation: Supports progressive difficulty based on student performance history Skill-Based Sequencing: Enables curriculum-aligned progression through related concepts Diversity and Engagement: Incorporates randomization to maintain student interest 3.7 Integration with Gemma LLM for Response Generation The Gemma Large Language Model generates contextually appropriate responses: Prompt Construction: Dynamically incorporates: Role definition establishing Gemma as a patient tutor Mode-specific instructions based on predicted tutor mode Retrieved question-answer context from knowledge bank Original student query for context Mode-Specific Prompting: Explanation Mode: Instructs detailed, step-by-step breakdowns with examples Knowledge Mode: Directs concise, factual responses without elaboration Socratic Mode: Guides question-based teaching promoting self-discovery Quality Control: Post-generation filtering ensures appropriate length and content alignment 4. Results and Discussion 4.1 Quantitative Performance Metrics Overall Classification Performance: These findings represent impressive performance of the system on each of the classification tasks. The tutor mode classifier is validated with 97% accuracy, which suggest a good discrimination among the three pedagogical modes (Explanation, Knowledge and Socratic. Experimental results The topic classifier attains 95% accuracy in average of test cases across various academic subjects, which verifies its strength as a multi-class classification model. The performance is satisfying for both tasks since all of the precision and recall values from these two models are well-balanced without paying any card on excellent performance (no specific mode or topic).The high F1-scores confirm that the models maintain excellent performance across both majority and minority classes in the datasets, ensuring equitable treatment of all pedagogical modes and academic subjects. 4.2 System Performance Through Example Interactions Example 1 - Explanation Request Student Input: "Can you tell me how trains calculate speed?" Processing Pipeline: Tutor Mode: Explanation (confidence: 0.87) Topic: Physics - Kinematics (confidence: 0.92) Retrieved Question: "If a train travels 90 km in 2 hours, what is its average speed?" Gemma Response: "Let's work through this step by step to understand how we calculate a train's speed. Speed tells us how fast something is moving—specifically, how much distance it covers in a certain amount of time. The formula we use is: $$\text{Speed} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}}$$ Now, if a train travels 90 kilometers in 2 hours, we can substitute these values: $$\text{Speed} = \frac{90 \text{ km}}{2 \text{ hours}} = 45 \text{ km/h}$$ This means the train covers 45 kilometers every hour. Does this make sense?" Example 2 - Factual Knowledge Request Student Input: "What is photosynthesis?" Processing Pipeline: Tutor Mode: Knowledge (confidence: 0.94) Topic: Biology - Plant Systems (confidence: 0.96) Gemma Response: "Photosynthesis is the process by which plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy stored in glucose molecules. The basic equation is: 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{light energy} \rightarrow C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 In simpler terms, plants take in carbon dioxide and water, and with sunlight, produce glucose and oxygen." Example 3 - Socratic Engagement Student Input: "I don't understand why objects fall at the same rate regardless of mass" Processing Pipeline: Tutor Mode: Socratic (confidence: 0.82) Topic: Physics - Mechanics (confidence: 0.89) Gemma Response: "That's a great question that puzzled people for centuries! Let me ask you something: when you drop a heavy book and a light feather at the same time, which hits the ground first in normal conditions? Now, what if we removed all the air? What do you think would happen? Consider this: does gravity pull harder on heavier objects? And if it does, do heavier objects also have more inertia—more resistance to motion? How might these factors balance each other?" 4.3 Performance Across Different Domains High-Performance Areas: Mathematics and Physics: Strong performance due to precise technical vocabulary Computer Science: Effective classification aided by distinctive terminology Moderate-Performance Areas: Biology and Chemistry: Good performance with occasional sub-topic confusion History: Adequate performance with some temporal ambiguity challenges Improvement Opportunities: Interdisciplinary questions spanning multiple subjects Highly colloquial or abbreviated informal language 5. Conclusion The AI Powered Tutor is a successful an example of the combination of classical NLP preprocessing, machine learning classification, and generative AI. Exceptional performance of the system with 97% validation accuracy in detecting pedagogical mode and 95 percent test accuracy in classification of topics is validated by rigorous 100-epoch training with remarkable convergence properties. The dynamics of the training process bring out significant information: the topic classifier approaches convergence quickly in 20 epochs because of the presence of vocabulary related to unique subject material and the tutor mode classifier takes about 50 epochs to fully learn the more subtle patterns of the pedagogical intent. The models are also both remarkably stable once converged and show superb generalization, performance on a test/validation run is comparable or better than training run performance being a testament to good regularization strategies. The two-stage classification architecture ensures responses are both topic-specific and pedagogy-aware, creating an interactive learning experience that rivals human tutors in adaptability. The extremely low test loss values (~0.02-0.03 for topic classification, ~0.08 for mode classification) indicate well-calibrated probability estimates, making the system's predictions highly reliable for educational deployment. Such a hybrid architecture demonstrates that good AI in education does not need the state-of-the-art models in each of its parts. Careful combination of the classical methods of NLP with contemporary methods of classification and generative AI outputs become powerful and computer-efficiency results that can be deployed in large-scale institutions. The production-ready performance measures (>95% accuracy in both tasks) and the convergence properties of the system reveal its suitability to be implemented in the real educational practice. 6. Future Scope 6.1 Short-Term Enhancements Adaptation in Real Time: Incorporate feedback loops for dynamic adaptation of tutoring modes to the learner’s responses and performance regularities. Better Question Selection: Use Smart Sequencing based on Student’s History, Skill Dependencies and Best Difficulty Path. Multilingual Preprocessing: Expand preprocessing pipelines to Spanish, Mandarin, Hindi and other key educational languages. 6.2 Medium-Term Developments Emotion and Sentiment Detection: Incorporate affective computing to detect frustration or confusion and adjust teaching approaches accordingly. Dialogue State Tracking: Implement conversation memory for coherent multi-turn dialogues with contextual continuity. Personalized Learning Profiles: Develop comprehensive student models tracking knowledge mastery, preferences, and optimal teaching modes. 6.3 Long-Term Vision Curriculum Mapping: Align with educational standards and frameworks for consistent course alignment. Collaborative Learning: Facilitate peer learning situations where a number of students can interact at the same time. Multimodal Interaction: Introduce visual learning with diagrams, video suggestions and interactive simulations, as well as voice interaction. Teacher Dashboard: Create teacher interfaces -- to keep track of progress, struggles and analytics at the classroom level. Research Platform: Facilitate educational research through collection of anonymized data on learning strategies and efficacy. 7. References Anderson, J. R., et al. (2021). text: Synopsis ReWaIn-MTS-Cap: A Vision-Language Dataset and Benchmark for Mexican Traffic Sign Detection and Captioning By ROHAN KUMAR [BT23CSA013] JWALIT LAL [BT23CSA027] NISHANT SINGH [BT23CSA048] UJJWAL PRAKASH [BT23CSA058] Semester-6, Branch-CSA Course Name: MINI PROJECT - II [CSD302] Under the guidance of DR. JAGDISH CHAKOLE Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Information Technology, Nagpur 2026 - 2027 Problem Statement Traffic sign detection has been a well-researched domain, with datasets available for German, Chinese, and American traffic signs. However, Mexican traffic signs remain significantly underrepresented in the research community. The ReWaIn-MTS dataset (published in IEEE Latin America Transactions, 2025) addressed detection by providing 1,439 images with 2,283 bounding-box annotations across 37 sign classes from eight Mexican cities. Despite this progress, no dataset currently exists that enables natural language understanding of Mexican traffic signs. Existing detection systems can only answer 'What is the object?' and 'Where is it?' — they cannot explain what a sign means, describe a scene in natural language, or communicate with a driver in human-readable form. This gap limits the development of explainable and accessible driver assistance systems for the Latin American region. Objectives Extend the ReWaIn-MTS detection dataset with natural language captions to create the first Mexican Traffic Sign Captioning Dataset. Generate three structurally diverse captions per image covering object-meaning, scene- context, and driver-instruction perspectives. Establish a benchmark by fine-tuning vision-language models on the proposed dataset. Evaluate model performance using standard captioning metrics: BLEU, CIDEr, METEOR, and ROUGE. Demonstrate the utility of the dataset for explainable driver assistance systems. Dataset Contribution The proposed dataset, ReWaIn-MTS-Cap, extends the existing ReWaIn-MTS detection dataset by adding structured natural language annotations. The dataset contributes the following: 1,439 road images sourced from eight Mexican cities covering diverse urban environments. Three human-written captions per image, each following a distinct template: (1) Object + Meaning focused, (2) Scene + Context focused, and (3) Driver Instruction focused. Total of approximately 4,317 captions covering all 37 traffic sign classes. Captions include sign type, regulatory meaning, speed values, spatial relationships, and scene context — all critical for driving assistance reasoning. Dataset released in COCO-style JSON format, directly compatible with HuggingFace, BLIP, and ViT-GPT2 training pipelines. Methodology The project follows a four-phase methodology: Phase 1 — Dataset Preparation The original YOLO-format bounding box labels are parsed to identify all traffic sign classes present in each image. A class map is constructed linking each class ID to its official sign name and regulatory meaning as per the Mexican Road Signaling Manual. Phase 2 — Caption Annotation Four annotators independently caption assigned image subsets using a standardized three-template guideline. Each template targets a distinct use case: object identification with meaning, scene-level description, and driver-facing instruction. A pre-annotation calibration exercise ensures consistency across annotators before the full dataset is captioned. Phase 3 — Dataset Formatting and Validation Captions are collected via a shared Google Sheet and converted to COCO-style JSON using an automated Python script. A validation pass ensures every image has exactly three captions, all sign classes are mentioned, speed values are consistent across captions, and no vague or irrelevant content is present. Phase 4 — Model Training and Evaluation Pre-trained vision-language models are fine-tuned on the proposed dataset. Model performance is measured using BLEU-1, BLEU-4, CIDEr, METEOR, and ROUGE-L metrics. Qualitative analysis is conducted to verify that generated captions correctly identify sign types, meanings, and scene context. Tools and Technologies Expected Results and Outcomes A publicly released captioning dataset (ReWaIn-MTS-Cap) with ~4,317 human-written captions across 1,439 images. A fine-tuned BLIP or ViT-GPT2 model capable of generating meaningful natural language descriptions of Mexican traffic sign scenes. Baseline evaluation scores on BLEU, CIDEr, and METEOR metrics establishing a benchmark for future research. A research paper demonstrating the dataset construction methodology, annotation guidelines, and model evaluation results. A reusable annotation pipeline applicable to other regional traffic sign datasets globally. Novelty and Research Significance This project is the first to introduce a dedicated image captioning dataset for Mexican traffic signs. While detection datasets describe object locations, captioning datasets enable semantic understanding — bridging the gap between computer vision and natural language processing. The proposed dataset supports research in explainable AI, vision-language modeling, and intelligent transportation systems for the Latin American context, an area currently underserved by existing benchmarks. References D. Bolanos-Flores et al., "Toward a New Dataset: Mexican Traffic Signs ReWaIn-MTS for Detection," IEEE Latin America Transactions, Vol. 23, No. 7, July 2025. Secretaría de Infraestructura, Comunicaciones y Transportes, "Manual de Señalización Vial y Dispositivos de Seguridad," 2014. J. Li et al., "BLIP: Bootstrapping Language-Image Pre-training," ICML, 2022. A. Vaswani et al., "Attention Is All You Need," NeurIPS, 2017.

text: INDIAN INSTITUTE OF INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY, NAGPUR Department of Computer Science and Engineering Computer Vision Techniques Practical Laboratory Notebook Index of Experiments Camera Opening Camera streaming Via Phone RGB to Gray RBG to Cmyk RGB to Cyan Quantized Image Naive average blur Gaussian Blur Canny edge detection Naive sampling Log Dog Experiment 1 Title: Camera Opening Problem Statement: Stream camera live video with python code Key Concept: OpenCV VideoCapture() function Real-time frame reading and display Webcam initialization and release Handling video stream termination with keyboard events Software / Hardware Setup: Software: Python 3.10+, OpenCV Library Hardware: Built-in or USB Webcam CPU: Intel Core i5 or above RAM: >= 8 GB OS: Windows 10 / 11 IDE: VS Code / Jupyter Notebook Code: import cv2 # 0 = Default webcam, 1 or 2 if you have external cameras cap = cv2.VideoCapture(0) print(cv2.__version__) if not cap.isOpened(): print("Error: Could not open webcam") exit() while True: ret, frame = cap.read() if not ret: print("Failed to grab frame") break cv2.imshow("Webcam Feed", frame) if cv2.waitKey(1) & 0xFF == ord('q'): break cap.release() cv2.destroyAllWindows() Output: Observation, Errors & Learning: The webcam successfully opened and started capturing frames. Each frame appeared in real-time with minimal delay. When ‘q’ was pressed, the stream terminated smoothly. Frame rate may slightly vary depending on lighting and camera resolution. Conclusion: In this experiment, we successfully used OpenCV’s VideoCapture() function to access the webcam and display a real-time video stream. We learned how to manage continuous frame reading and safely terminate the feed using keyboard input Experiment :2 Title: Accessing and Displaying Live Video Feed from a Mobile IP Camera using OpenCV Problem Statement: To develop a Python program that connects to a mobile phone camera (acting as an IP webcam) through its local IP address and displays a live video feed using OpenCV. Key Concepts: IP camera streaming over a local network Accessing video sources through URLs OpenCV’s VideoCapture() for network streams Real-time frame reading and display Handling stream disconnection and termination Software / Hardware Setup: Software: Python 3.10+ OpenCV (opencv-python library) IP Webcam app installed on Andriod Phone Hardware: Android phone Laptop/PC and Phone connected to the same Wi-Fi network CPU: Intel Core i5 or higher RAM: 8 GB or higher OS: Windows 10 / 11 IDE: VS Code / Jupyter Notebook Code: print("hello") import cv2 video_source="http://192.168.137.62:8080/video" cap=cv2.VideoCapture(video_source) print(cv2.__version__) if not cap.isOpened(): print("error") exit() while True: ret,frame=cap.read() if not ret: print("stream ended or cannot catch fra,me") break cv2.imshow("Video feed",frame) if(cv2.waitKey(1) & 0xFF==ord('q')): break cap.release() cv2.destroyAllWindows() Output: Observation, Errors & Learning: Observation: The IP camera successfully streamed real-time video from the phone to the computer. Frame rate depended on network strength and Wi-Fi speed. Minimal delay was observed between the phone camera and displayed frame. Pressing the ‘q’ key terminated the stream gracefully. Errors Learning Outcomes: Understood how to connect OpenCV with network-based camera feeds (IP streams). Learned to use OpenCV’s VideoCapture() with URLs instead of hardware camera indices. Learned to handle frame reading, real-time display, and termination control. Gained insights into the role of network reliability and latency in live video processing. This serves as a foundation for advanced applications like remote surveillance, IoT camera monitoring, and real-time object detection from mobile devices. Conclusion: In this experiment, we successfully accessed and displayed live video feed from a mobile IP camera using OpenCV. The experiment demonstrated how OpenCV can interact with external devices over a network, providing a flexible and efficient way to integrate mobile cameras for real-time computer vision applications. Experiment 3 Title: Image Color Space Conversion: RGB to Grayscale Problem Statement: Write the given problem statement / Aim here - To implement an algorithm for converting a color image (represented in the RGB color space) into a grayscale image using the weighted average method. Key Concepts: RGB Color Model: Understanding how colors are represented by a combination of Red, Green, and Blue intensity values. Grayscale Conversion: The process of mapping a 3-channel (RGB) image to a single-channel (intensity) image. * Luminosity/Weighted Average Method: Using a specific formula (e.g., $0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$) to calculate the perceived brightness (luminance) for the grayscale value. NumPy Array Manipulation: Handling image data as multi-dimensional arrays. Image Libraries: Using PIL (Pillow) for image loading and Matplotlib for visualization. Software / Hardware Setup: List of Software & PC Specs - One: Python 3.x (Specify version if needed) Two: Libraries: NumPy, Pillow (PIL), Matplotlib Three: Operating System (e.g., Windows 11/Ubuntu 22.04) and PC Specs (e.g., 8GB RAM, Core i5 Code : import numpy as np from PIL import Image import matplotlib.pyplot as plt img = Image.open('Photo/cdc-IFpQtennlj8-unsplash.jpg ') blank = np.zeros((400,455),dtype='uint8') img_array = np.random.randint(0,255,(10,10,3),dtype='uint8') plt.figure(figsize=(8,6)) plt.imshow(img_array,cmap='gray',vmin=0,vmax=255) row,column,depth = img_array.shape for i in range(row): for j in range(column): R, G, B = img_array[i, j] text = f"({R},{G},{B})" brightness = (0.299*R + 0.587*G + 0.114*B) plt.text( j, i, text, ha='center', va='center', color='yellow' if np.mean([R,G,B]) < 128 else "black", fontsize=6 ) image_arr = np.array(image) if len(image_arr.shape) == 3 and image_arr.shape[2] == 3: R, G, B = image_arr[:,:,0], image_arr[:,:,1], image_arr[:,:,2] # Apply the luminosity method formula for grayscale conversion gray_arr = (0.299*R + 0.587*G + 0.114*B).astype(np.uint8) else: gray_arr = image_arr plt.figure(figsize=(10,5)) plt.subplot(1, 2, 1) plt.imshow(image_arr) plt.title("RGB Image") plt.axis("off") plt.axis("off") plt.show() # image = Image.fromarray(img_array) # image.save("random.jpg") # cv.waitKey(0) Output: Observation, Errors & Learning: Observation: The shape (853, 1280, 3) confirms the image is an array of 853 rows, 1280 columns, with 3 color channels (RGB). The resulting grayscale image successfully represents the original image's brightness without color. Challenge/Errors: Initial attempts might result in incorrect color balance if the weighted average (luminosity) formula isn't used correctly. Also, ensuring the data type is properly cast to np.uint8 is crucial to prevent value overflow or incorrect display of intensity levels (0-255). Learning: I learned that grayscale conversion is not a simple average, but a weighted average that accounts for the human eye's varying sensitivity to Red, Green, and Blue light (Green contributes the most to perceived brightness). Conclusion: This experiment successfully implemented the weighted average (luminosity) method to convert a color image from the RGB color space to a single-channel grayscale image. The technique demonstrated the fundamental computer vision principle of color space transformation for simplified image processing. Experiment 4 Title: RGB image to CMYK Problem Statement: To manually convert a digital image from the RGB color model to the CMYK color model using Pillow and NumPy, and visualize all channels using Matplotlib. Software / Hardware Setup: Software: Python 3.10+, Pillow, NumPy, Matplotlib Hardware: CPU: Intel Core i5 or higher RAM: 8 GB or higher OS: Windows 10 / 11 IDE: VS Code / Jupyter Notebook Code : import numpy as np from PIL import Image import matplotlib.pyplot as plt img = Image.open("Photo/zhong-liguo-cNLQ052nhvc-unsplash.jpg").convert("RGB") rgb = np.asarray(img).astype(float) / 255.0 r = rgb[..., 0] g = rgb[..., 1] b = rgb[..., 2] k = 1 - np.maximum.reduce([r, g, b]) # Compute C, M, Y channels using formula c = (1 - r - k) / (1 - k + 1e-8) m = (1 - g - k) / (1 - k + 1e-8) y = (1 - b - k) / (1 - k + 1e-8) #Combine CMYK channels into one image array (scaled back to 0–255) cmyk = np.dstack((c, m, y, k)) cmyk_img = (cmyk * 255).astype(np.uint8) fig1, axes1 = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 5)) axes1[0].imshow(img) axes1[0].set_title("Original RGB Image") axes1[0].axis("off") axes1[1].imshow(cmyk_img) axes1[1].set_title("Converted (CMYK Composite View)") axes1[1].axis("off") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() # --------------- FIGURE 2: Individual CMYK Channels ---------------- fig2, axes2 = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(10, 10)) axes2[0, 0].imshow(c, cmap="gray") axes2[0, 0].set_title("Cyan Channel") axes2[0, 0].axis("off") axes2[0, 1].imshow(m, cmap="gray") axes2[0, 1].set_title("Magenta Channel") axes2[0, 1].axis("off") axes2[1, 0].imshow(y, cmap="gray") axes2[1, 0].set_title("Yellow Channel") axes2[1, 0].axis("off") axes2[1, 1].imshow(k, cmap="gray") axes2[1, 1].set_title("Black (Key) Channel") axes2[1, 1].axis("off") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() Output Observation: The Cyan channel shows bright regions where the image has less red. The Magenta channel corresponds to green content reduction. The Yellow channel shows the blue content’s negative areas. The Black channel highlights dark intensity regions common to all RGB channels. The CMYK decomposition matches how printers layer color inks. Errors: Learning Outcomes: Understood RGB and CMYK color models and their mathematical relationship. Learned to manually compute CMYK values using NumPy. Gained experience in visualizing multiple color channels using Matplotlib. Observed the real-world connection between digital color representation and printing process. Conclusion: In this experiment, an RGB image was successfully converted to the CMYK color model using Pillow and NumPy, and the resulting channels were visualized using Matplotlib. The experiment demonstrated how CMYK represents colors in terms of ink absorption, complementing the additive RGB model used in digital imaging. Experiment 5 Title: Conversion of an Image from RGB to Cyan Component using Pillow and Matplotlib Problem Statement: To extract the Cyan component from a digital image by manually converting it from the RGB color model using Pillow and NumPy, and to visualize the original and cyan images using Matplotlib. Key Concepts: RGB color model and its components Relationship between RGB and CMY color models Cyan channel extraction formula: C = 1 - R Image manipulation using Pillow and NumPy Image visualization using Matplotlib Software / Hardware Setup: List of Software & PC Specs -  Software: Python 3.10+, Pillow, NumPy, Matplotlib  Hardware: CPU: Intel Core i5 or higher RAM: 8 GB or higher OS: Windows 10 / 11 IDE: VS Code / Jupyter Notebook Code import numpy as np from PIL import Image import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # Step 1: Load the image using Pillow and convert to RGB img = Image.open("Photo/zhong-liguo-cNLQ052nhvc-unsplash.jpg").convert("RGB") # Step 2: Convert image to NumPy array and normalize to [0, 1] rgb = np.asarray(img).astype(float) / 255.0 # Step 3: Extract the Red channel r = rgb[..., 0] # Step 4: Compute the Cyan component using the formula cyan = 1 - r # Step 5: Scale back to 0–255 and convert to image cyan_img = Image.fromarray((cyan * 255).astype(np.uint8)) # Step 6: Display Original and Cyan Images using Matplotlib fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 5)) axes[0].imshow(img) axes[0].set_title("Original RGB Image") axes[0].axis("off") axes[1].imshow(cyan_img, cmap="gray") axes[1].set_title("Cyan Component Image") axes[1].axis("off") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() Output: Observation: The cyan channel highlights regions that contain less red color in the image. The brightness in the cyan image is inversely proportional to the red intensity in the original image. The conversion formula C = 1 - R effectively captures cyan tones from the RGB representation. Errors (in Sentence Form): During this experiment, one common issue was the incorrect image path, which led to a FileNotFoundError . This was resolved by verifying the image’s correct directory location. Another issue observed was that the cyan image appeared inverted (dark regions instead of bright) when the formula C = R was mistakenly used instead of C = 1 - R. Correcting this equation fixed the output. Additionally, in some cases, the image looked completely white because the RGB array was not normalized to a 0–1 range before applying the formula. Proper normalization resolved this error. Learning Outcomes: Understood how Cyan color is derived as the complement of the Red channel. Learned to manually compute individual color components using NumPy operations. Gained experience in manipulating and visualizing images using Pillow and Matplotlib. Recognized the relationship between additive (RGB) and subtractive (CMY) color models. Conclusion: In this experiment, the Cyan component of an image was successfully extracted from the RGB color model using a manual mathematical approach with Pillow and NumPy. The result demonstrated how complementary color channels can be computed directly from RGB values, deepening understanding of color space conversions in computer vision. Experiment 6 Title: Image Quantization (Up and Down Quantization) using Pillow and Matplotlib Problem Statement: To perform image quantization by reducing and increasing the number of gray levels in an image (down-quantization and up-quantization) using Pillow, NumPy, and Matplotlib. Key Concepts: Digital image intensity representation (0–255 gray levels) Quantization process — mapping continuous intensity values to discrete levels Down-quantization (reducing bit depth) Up-quantization (increasing or restoring intensity levels) Visualization using Matplotlib Software / Hardware Setup: Software: Python 3.10+, Pillow, NumPy, Matplotlib Hardware: CPU: Intel Core i5 or higher RAM: 8 GB or higher OS: Windows 10 / 11 IDE: VS Code / Jupyter Notebook Code : import numpy as np from PIL import Image import matplotlib.pyplot as plt img = Image.open("Photo/zhong-liguo-cNLQ052nhvc-unsplash.jpg").convert("L") # Convert image to NumPy array img_array = np.array(img) # Define quantization function def quantize_image(image, levels): """ Quantizes the image to a given number of gray levels. """ max_val = 255 step = max_val // (levels - 1) quantized = (image // step) * step return quantized.astype(np.uint8) #Perform down and up quantization down_quantized = quantize_image(img_array, levels=4) # Down-quantization (4 gray levels) up_quantized = quantize_image(img_array, levels=16) # Up-quantization (16 gray levels) # Display original, down-quantized, and up-quantized images fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 6)) axes[0].imshow(img_array, cmap='gray') axes[0].set_title("Original Grayscale Image") axes[0].axis("off") axes[1].imshow(down_quantized, cmap='gray') axes[1].set_title("Down Quantized (4 Levels)") axes[1].axis("off") axes[2].imshow(up_quantized, cmap='gray') axes[2].set_title("Up Quantized (16 Levels)") axes[2].axis("off") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() Output: Observation: As the number of quantization levels decreases, the image loses detail and smoothness, producing a posterized effect. When quantization levels increase, the image gradually approaches its original form with finer intensity differences. Down-quantization compresses data but sacrifices quality, while up-quantization increases gray levels but cannot restore lost detail beyond the original. Learning Outcomes: Understood the concept of image quantization and how it reduces the number of intensity levels. Learned to implement both down-quantization (reducing levels) and up-quantization (restoring levels). Observed how quantization affects image quality and compression trade-offs. Developed skills in NumPy-based mathematical manipulation of image data and visualization using Matplotlib. Conclusion: In this experiment, up and down quantization were performed successfully using Pillow and NumPy. The experiment demonstrated how changing the number of intensity levels affects image detail and smoothness. Down-quantization results in noticeable quality loss, while up-quantization provides smoother transitions but cannot fully recover original details. This illustrates the importance of quantization in image compression and signal representation in computer vision. Experiment 7 Title: Image Spatial Filtering: Naive Average (Mean) Blurring Problem Statement: To implement a basic spatial filter, specifically the average (mean) blur, using a sliding window and replicate padding, to smooth and reduce noise in a color image. Key Concepts: Names of Key concepts you think you learnt in this experiment Software / Hardware Setup: List of Software & PC Specs - One: Python 3.x Two: Libraries: NumPy, Pillow (PIL), Matplotlib Three: Operating System and PC Specs Code : from PIL import Image import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt def naive_average_blur(image_path, radius=1): # Open the image and convert to RGB (for consistency) img = Image.open(image_path).convert("RGB") width, height = img.size blurred_img = Image.new("RGB", (width, height)) pixels = img.load() new_pixels = blurred_img.load() kernel_size = 2 * radius + 1 num_neighbors = kernel_size * kernel_size for x in range(radius, width - radius): for y in range(radius, height - radius): # Variables to store sum of R, G, B values for the neighborhood r_sum, g_sum, b_sum = 0, 0, 0 # Iterate over the kernel neighborhood for dx in range(-radius, radius + 1): for dy in range(-radius, radius + 1): nx, ny = x + dx, y + dy try: r, g, b = pixels[nx, ny] r_sum += r g_sum += g b_sum += b except IndexError: # Handle edge cases (this simple loop avoids them) pass r_avg = r_sum // num_neighbors g_avg = g_sum // num_neighbors b_avg = b_sum // num_neighbors # Set the new pixel value in the blurred image copy new_pixels[x, y] = (r_avg, g_avg, b_avg) return blurred_img input_image_path = 'Photo/cdc-IFpQtennlj8-unsplash.jpg' blurred_image = naive_average_blur(input_image_path, radius=5) original_image = Image.open(input_image_path) # 4. Display images using Matplotlib fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 6)) # Display original image axes[0].imshow(original_image) axes[0].set_title("Original Image") axes[0].axis('off') # Hide axis ticks # Display blurred image axes[1].imshow(blurred_image) axes[1].set_title(f"Blurred Image (Radius 5)") axes[1].axis('off') # Hide axis ticks # Show the plot window plt.tight_layout() plt.show() Output: Observation, Errors & Learning: The blurred image appears smoother, with sharp edges and noise significantly reduced. Increasing the kernel size (e.g., $5 \times 5$ vs. $3 \times 3$) results in a more pronounced blurring effect. Correctly handling the padding to ensure the output image size matches the input image size was essential. Miscalculating the padding size (pad = (ksize - 1) // 2) can lead to dimension errors. The naive implementation using nested loops is computationally slow. The average filter is a basic low-pass filter that attenuates high-frequency components (like noise and sharp details). Using NumPy's array operations for mean calculation is a necessary step, but for performance, optimized convolution methods (like those in OpenCV) are usually preferred over naive nested loops. Conclusion: Conclusion of the experiment in your words. The experiment successfully implemented the average blur spatial filter using a naive iterative approach with replicate padding. This demonstrated the core mechanism of spatial domain filtering, where the value of each pixel is determined by the average intensity of its neighbors, resulting in image smoothing. Experiment 8 Title:RGC to CYAN Implementation of Gaussian Blur using a Custom Kernel in Python Problem Statement : To implement the Gaussian Blur operation manually using a Gaussian kernel and convolution operation in Python, and compare the result with the original image. Key Concepts: Image smoothing and noise reduction Gaussian function and kernel generation Convolution operation on images Pixel-level manipulation using NumPy arrays Visualization using Matplotlib Software / Hardware Setup: Software: Python 3.10+, NumPy, Pillow (PIL), Matplotlib Hardware: CPU: Intel Core i5 or above RAM: 8 GB or higher OS: Windows 10 / 11 IDE: VS Code / Jupyter Notebook Code: import numpy as np from PIL import Image import cv2 def rgb2gray(img): """Convert RGB image to grayscale.""" return np.dot(img[...,:3], [0.2989, 0.5870, 0.1140]) def gaussian_kernel(size=5, sigma=1): """Generate a Gaussian kernel.""" ax = np.linspace(-(size // 2), size // 2, size) xx, yy = np.meshgrid(ax, ax) kernel = np.exp(-(xx**2 + yy**2) / (2. * sigma**2)) return kernel / np.sum(kernel) def convolve(img, kernel): """Apply convolution between image and kernel.""" kh, kw = kernel.shape pad_h = kh // 2 pad_w = kw // 2 padded = np.pad(img, ((pad_h, pad_h), (pad_w, pad_w)), mode='constant') output = np.zeros_like(img) for i in range(img.shape[0]): for j in range(img.shape[1]): region = padded[i:i+kh, j:j+kw] output[i, j] = np.sum(region * kernel) return output def gradient_intensity(img): """Compute image gradient and direction using Sobel filters.""" Kx = np.array([[-1, 0, 1], [-2, 0, 2], [-1, 0, 1]]) Ky = np.array([[1, 2, 1], [0, 0, 0], [-1, -2, -1]]) Ix = convolve(img, Kx) Iy = convolve(img, Ky) G = np.hypot(Ix, Iy) # Gradient magnitude G = G / G.max() * 255 theta = np.arctan2(Iy, Ix) # Gradient direction return (G, theta) def non_max_suppression(G, theta): """Thin out the edges.""" M, N = G.shape Z = np.zeros((M, N), dtype=np.int32) angle = theta * 180. / np.pi angle[angle < 0] += 180 for i in range(1, M-1): for j in range(1, N-1): q = 255 r = 255 # Angle 0 if (0 <= angle[i,j] < 22.5) or (157.5 <= angle[i,j] <= 180): q = G[i, j+1] r = G[i, j-1] # Angle 45 elif (22.5 <= angle[i,j] < 67.5): q = G[i+1, j-1] r = G[i-1, j+1] # Angle 90 elif (67.5 <= angle[i,j] < 112.5): q = G[i+1, j] r = G[i-1, j] # Angle 135 elif (112.5 <= angle[i,j] < 157.5): q = G[i-1, j-1] r = G[i+1, j+1] if (G[i,j] >= q) and (G[i,j] >= r): Z[i,j] = G[i,j] else: Z[i,j] = 0 return Z def threshold(img, low_ratio=0.05, high_ratio=0.15): """Apply double thresholding.""" high_threshold = img.max() * high_ratio low_threshold = high_threshold * low_ratio M, N = img.shape res = np.zeros((M,N), dtype=np.int32) weak = 25 strong = 255 strong_i, strong_j = np.where(img >= high_threshold) weak_i, weak_j = np.where((img <= high_threshold) & (img >= low_threshold)) res[strong_i, strong_j] = strong res[weak_i, weak_j] = weak return (res, weak, strong) def hysteresis(img, weak=25, strong=255): """Track edges by hysteresis.""" M, N = img.shape for i in range(1, M-1): for j in range(1, N-1): if img[i,j] == weak: if ((img[i+1, j-1] == strong) or (img[i+1, j] == strong) or (img[i+1, j+1] == strong) or (img[i, j-1] == strong) or (img[i, j+1] == strong) or (img[i-1, j-1] == strong) or (img[i-1, j] == strong) or (img[i-1, j+1] == strong)): img[i, j] = strong else: img[i, j] = 0 return img def canny_edge_detection(img_path): """Complete Canny edge detection pipeline.""" img = Image.open(img_path).convert('RGB') img = np.array(img) gray = rgb2gray(img) blur = convolve(gray, gaussian_kernel(size=5, sigma=1)) G, theta = gradient_intensity(blur) non_max = non_max_suppression(G, theta) threshold_img, weak, strong = threshold(non_max) result = hysteresis(threshold_img, weak, strong) return result import matplotlib.pyplot as plt edge_result = canny_edge_detection('Photo/Friends.jpg') plt.imshow(edge_result, cmap='gray') plt.title('Canny Edge Detection') plt.axis('off') plt.show() Output: Observation: The manually created Gaussian kernel successfully blurred the image. Increasing the kernel size or sigma increased the degree of smoothness. The convolution-based implementation took slightly longer compared to built-in OpenCV functions. The output demonstrated reduced image noise and smoother color transitions. Learning Outcomes: Learned to manually generate a Gaussian kernel using mathematical equations. Understood the concept of 2D convolution applied on image matrices. Understood how sigma and kernel size affect the amount of blur. Gained insight into how OpenCV’s built-in cv2.GaussianBlur() works internally. Strengthened understanding of low-pass filtering and image smoothing in computer vision. Conclusion: In this experiment, a custom Gaussian blur function was implemented using NumPy and convolution operations. The results proved that Gaussian filtering effectively smooths an image by reducing high-frequency noise, thus demonstrating the foundational concept of image smoothing in computer vision. Experiment 9 Title: Image Edge Detection: Canny Algorithm Problem Statement: Write the given problem statement / Aim here - To implement the multi-stage Canny Edge Detection algorithm to identify and localize strong edges in a grayscale image while minimizing noise interference and false edges. Key Concepts: Names of Key concepts you think you learnt in this experiment Key Concepts: Canny Edge Detection: An optimal edge detection technique known for its low error rate, good localization, and single response per edge. Gaussian Blurring: Used as a pre-processing step to smooth the image and remove noise before gradient calculation. Gradient Calculation: Determining the magnitude and direction of intensity changes (edges) in the image. Non-Maximum Suppression: Thinning the edges by preserving only the local maxima of the gradient magnitude. Hysteresis Thresholding: Using two thresholds (high and low) to classify pixels as strong, weak, or non-edges and connecting weak edges to strong ones. Software / Hardware Setup: List of Software & PC Specs - One: Python 3.x (Specify version if needed) Two: Libraries: NumPy, Pillow (PIL), Matplotlib Three: Operating System (e.g., Windows 11/Ubuntu 22.04) and PC Specs (e.g., 8GB RAM, Core i5 Code # Experiment 5: Manual Gaussian Blur + Canny Edge Detection import numpy as np from PIL import Image import cv2 import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # Step 1: Load and convert image img = Image.open('Photo/cdc-IFpQtennlj8-unsplash.jpg').convert("RGB") # Step 2: Convert image to grayscale gray_img = img.convert("L") gray_array = np.array(gray_img, dtype=np.float32) # Step 3: Create Gaussian kernel manually def gaussian_kernel(size=5, sigma=1.4): ax = np.linspace(-(size // 2), size // 2, size) xx, yy = np.meshgrid(ax, ax) kernel = np.exp(-(xx**2 + yy**2) / (2 * sigma**2)) return kernel / np.sum(kernel) # Step 4: Apply convolution manually to blur image def apply_convolution(image, kernel): h, w = image.shape k = kernel.shape[0] r = k // 2 output = np.zeros_like(image) for i in range(r, h - r): for j in range(r, w - r): region = image[i - r:i + r + 1, j - r:j + r + 1] output[i, j] = np.sum(region * kernel) return output # Generate and apply Gaussian Blur kernel = gaussian_kernel(size=5, sigma=1.4) blurred_array = apply_convolution(gray_array, kernel) # Convert blurred array to uint8 blurred_array = np.clip(blurred_array, 0, 255).astype(np.uint8) # Step 5: Apply Canny Edge Detection (only edge detection from OpenCV) edges = cv2.Canny(blurred_array, threshold1=50, threshold2=150) # Step 6: Display Results using Matplotlib fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 6)) axes[0].imshow(img) axes[0].set_title("Original Image") axes[0].axis("off") axes[1].imshow(blurred_array, cmap='gray') axes[1].set_title("Manually Blurred Image") axes[1].axis("off") axes[2].imshow(edges, cmap='gray') axes[2].set_title("Canny Edge Detection Result") axes[2].axis("off") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() Output: Observation, Errors & Learning: Observation: The shape (853, 1280, 3) confirms the image is an array of 853 rows, 1280 columns, with 3 color channels (RGB). The resulting grayscale image successfully represents the original image's brightness without color. Challenge/Errors: Initial attempts might result in incorrect color balance if the weighted average (luminosity) formula isn't used correctly. Also, ensuring the data type is properly cast to np.uint8 is crucial to prevent value overflow or incorrect display of intensity levels (0-255). Learning: I learned that grayscale conversion is not a simple average, but a weighted average that accounts for the human eye's varying sensitivity to Red, Green, and Blue light (Green contributes the most to perceived brightness). Conclusion In this experiment, Gaussian blur was implemented manually using Pillow and NumPy to smooth the image before performing Canny edge detection. The results proved that preprocessing significantly affects the accuracy and clarity of detected edges. This experiment provided an in-depth understanding of image smoothing, convolution, and edge detection fundamentals in computer vision. Experiment 10 Title: Naive Up-Sampling and Down-Sampling of an Image using Pillow and NumPy Problem Statement: To perform naive up-sampling and down-sampling on a digital image using Pillow and NumPy, without using any interpolation functions, to observe the effect of image scaling on quality and resolution. problem statement / Aim here - To implement a naive downsampling (subsampling) technique using array slicing to reduce the spatial resolution of an image, demonstrating the basic concept of image resizing. Key Concepts: Image resizing and resolution Down-sampling (reducing pixel count) Up-sampling (increasing pixel count) Nearest neighbor pixel duplication (naive scaling) Effect of sampling on image clarity and quality Software / Hardware Setup: Software: Python 3.10+, Pillow, NumPy, Matplotlib Hardware: CPU: Intel Core i5 or higher RAM: 8 GB or higher OS: Windows 10 / 11 IDE: VS Code / Jupyter Notebook Code: import numpy as np from PIL import Image import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # Step 1: Load image and convert to RGB img = Image.open("Photo/zhong-liguo-cNLQ052nhvc-unsplash.jpg").convert("RGB") # Convert to NumPy array img_array = np.array(img) # Step 2: Define naive down-sampling function def downsample(image, factor): """ Naive down-sampling: take every nth pixel. """ return image[::factor, ::factor] # Step 3: Define naive up-sampling function def upsample(image, factor): """ Naive up-sampling: repeat pixels by a given factor. """ return np.repeat(np.repeat(image, factor, axis=0), factor, axis=1) # Step 4: Perform naive down-sampling and up-sampling down_sampled = downsample(img_array, 4) # reduce resolution by 4x up_sampled = upsample(img_array, 2) # increase resolution by 2x # Step 5: Display results using Matplotlib fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 6)) axes[0].imshow(img_array) axes[0].set_title("Original Image") axes[0].axis("off") axes[1].imshow(down_sampled) axes[1].set_title("Naive Down-Sampled (x4 Reduced)") axes[1].axis("off") axes[2].imshow(up_sampled) axes[2].set_title("Naive Up-Sampled (x2 Enlarged)") axes[2].axis("off") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() Output: Observation, Errors & Learning: Write your observations, mention the challenges you faced during performance of this experiment. Also, mention the key learnings that you took from this experiment. Observation: In down-sampling, removing every nth pixel caused a significant reduction in resolution, leading to loss of detail. The up-sampled image became larger, but the pixel replication made it appear blocky and unnatural. The process confirmed that naive sampling methods do not interpolate or smooth pixels, resulting in rough scaling artifacts. Image clarity and sharpness directly depend on the scaling technique used — naive methods are computationally simple but visually inferior. Challenge/Errors: The use of astype(np.uint8) is necessary to ensure the downsampled array is correctly interpreted as an image (0-255 range) by Pillow and Matplotlib. Ensuring the step size is applied correctly across the first two dimensions (height and width) was key. Learning: I learned that while direct subsampling using array slicing is simple and fast, it is a poor method for resizing because it causes aliasing by discarding high-frequency information. Proper downsampling requires applying a low-pass filter (anti-aliasing filter) before subsampling to smooth the image first. Conclusion: In this experiment, naive up-sampling and down-sampling were performed successfully using Pillow and NumPy. The results demonstrated that while down-sampling reduces image detail and resolution, up-sampling increases image size but introduces blockiness. The experiment highlights the fundamental principles of image scaling and the trade-off between computational simplicity and visual quality in computer vision applications. Experiment 11 Title: Edge Detection using the Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG) Operator Implemented with Pillow and NumPy Problem Statement: To implement the Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG) edge detection technique using Pillow and NumPy, which involves smoothing the image with a Gaussian filter and then applying the Laplacian operator to identify edge regions. Key Concepts: Gaussian filtering for noise reduction Laplacian operator for second-order derivative edge detection LoG operator = ∇²(G * I) — combines smoothing and differentiation Zero-crossing detection to locate edges Visualization using Matplotlib Software / Hardware Setup: Software: Python 3.10+, Pillow, NumPy, Matplotlib Hardware: CPU: Intel Core i5 or above RAM: 8 GB or higher OS: Windows 10 / 11 IDE: VS Code / Jupyter Notebook Code: import numpy as np from PIL import Image import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # Step 1: Load and convert to grayscale img = Image.open("Photo/zhong-liguo-cNLQ052nhvc-unsplash.jpg").convert("L") img_array = np.array(img, dtype=float) # Step 2: Gaussian kernel generation def gaussian_kernel(size=5, sigma=1): ax = np.linspace(-(size // 2), size // 2, size) xx, yy = np.meshgrid(ax, ax) kernel = np.exp(-(xx**2 + yy**2) / (2 * sigma**2)) return kernel / np.sum(kernel) # Step 3: Convolution function def convolve(img, kernel): kh, kw = kernel.shape pad_h, pad_w = kh // 2, kw // 2 padded = np.pad(img, ((pad_h, pad_h), (pad_w, pad_w)), mode='constant') output = np.zeros_like(img) for i in range(img.shape[0]): for j in range(img.shape[1]): region = padded[i:i+kh, j:j+kw] output[i, j] = np.sum(region * kernel) return output # Step 4: Apply Gaussian smoothing blurred = convolve(img_array, gaussian_kernel(size=5, sigma=1)) # Step 5: Laplacian operator laplacian_kernel = np.array([[0, 1, 0], [1, -4, 1], [0, 1, 0]]) laplacian = convolve(blurred, laplacian_kernel) # Step 6: Zero-crossing detection def zero_crossing(img): zc_img = np.zeros_like(img) for i in range(1, img.shape[0]-1): for j in range(1, img.shape[1]-1): patch = img[i-1:i+2, j-1:j+2] p_min, p_max = patch.min(), patch.max() if p_min < 0 < p_max: zc_img[i, j] = 255 return zc_img edges = zero_crossing(laplacian) # Step 7: Display results fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 6)) axes[0].imshow(img_array, cmap='gray') axes[0].set_title("Original Grayscale Image") axes[0].axis("off") axes[1].imshow(laplacian, cmap='gray') axes[1].set_title("Laplacian of Gaussian (Filtered Image)") axes[1].axis("off") axes[2].imshow(edges, cmap='gray') axes[2].set_title("Detected Edges (Zero-Crossings)") axes[2].axis("off") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() Output: Experiment 5 Title: Second-Order Edge Detection: Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG) and Difference of Gaussians (DoG) Problem Statement: Write the given problem statement / Aim here - To implement and compare two second-order derivative edge detection techniques, Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG) and Difference of Gaussians (DoG), used to find zero-crossings, which accurately localize image edges after initial smoothing. Key Concepts: Names of Key concepts you think you learnt in this experiment Second-Order Edge Detectors: Operators (like LoG and DoG) that use the second derivative of the intensity function to detect edges, corresponding to points of zero-crossing. Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG): Applying the Laplacian operator (second derivative) to a Gaussian-smoothed image. This acts as a single operator that efficiently performs noise reduction and edge detection. Difference of Gaussians (DoG): An approximation of the LoG operator by subtracting two Gaussian-blurred versions of the image, each with a different standard deviation ($\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$). Zero-Crossing: The point where the second derivative crosses zero, indicating the center of an edge. Scale-Space Theory: The relationship between LoG and DoG, where DoG can efficiently compute features across different scales. Code : import numpy as np from PIL import Image import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # Step 1: Load and convert to grayscale img = Image.open("Photo/zhong-liguo-cNLQ052nhvc-unsplash.jpg").convert("L") img_array = np.array(img, dtype=float) # Step 2: Gaussian kernel generation def gaussian_kernel(size=5, sigma=1): ax = np.linspace(-(size // 2), size // 2, size) xx, yy = np.meshgrid(ax, ax) kernel = np.exp(-(xx**2 + yy**2) / (2 * sigma**2)) return kernel / np.sum(kernel) # Step 3: Convolution function def convolve(img, kernel): kh, kw = kernel.shape pad_h, pad_w = kh // 2, kw // 2 padded = np.pad(img, ((pad_h, pad_h), (pad_w, pad_w)), mode='constant') output = np.zeros_like(img) for i in range(img.shape[0]): for j in range(img.shape[1]): region = padded[i:i+kh, j:j+kw] output[i, j] = np.sum(region * kernel) return output # Step 4: Apply Gaussian smoothing blurred = convolve(img_array, gaussian_kernel(size=5, sigma=1)) # Step 5: Laplacian operator laplacian_kernel = np.array([[0, 1, 0], [1, -4, 1], [0, 1, 0]]) laplacian = convolve(blurred, laplacian_kernel) # Step 6: Zero-crossing detection def zero_crossing(img): zc_img = np.zeros_like(img) for i in range(1, img.shape[0]-1): for j in range(1, img.shape[1]-1): patch = img[i-1:i+2, j-1:j+2] p_min, p_max = patch.min(), patch.max() if p_min < 0 < p_max: zc_img[i, j] = 255 return zc_img edges = zero_crossing(laplacian) # Step 7: Display results fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 6)) axes[0].imshow(img_array, cmap='gray') axes[0].set_title("Original Grayscale Image") axes[0].axis("off") axes[1].imshow(laplacian, cmap='gray') axes[1].set_title("Laplacian of Gaussian (Filtered Image)") axes[1].axis("off") axes[2].imshow(edges, cmap='gray') axes[2].set_title("Detected Edges (Zero-Crossings)") axes[2].axis("off") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() Output: Observation, Errors & Learning Observation: Gaussian smoothing successfully reduced noise, ensuring smoother gradients. The Laplacian filter detected regions of rapid intensity change, representing potential edges. Zero-crossing detection accurately extracted edges from the Laplacian image. The output demonstrated symmetrical edges and strong localization but showed some sensitivity to noise in fine-texture regions. Errors (in Sentence Form): Initially, the output appeared completely black because the Laplacian kernel was not normalized properly; correcting its definition fixed the issue. Another error occurred when the image edges were clipped during convolution because padding was missing; adding appropriate zero-padding solved it. The zero-crossing detector initially produced too many false edges due to low Gaussian smoothing; increasing the sigma value to 1 reduced this noise. In some runs, a float overflow occurred during convolution with large kernel values, which was fixed by converting arrays to float before processing. Learning Outcomes: Learned how the Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG) combines smoothing and edge detection. Understood the concept of zero-crossings as indicators of edges. Practiced manual convolution operations and kernel design. Observed the trade-off between noise reduction and edge localization. Strengthened understanding of second-order derivative-based edge detection. Conclusion: In this experiment, the Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG) operator was implemented from scratch using Pillow and NumPy. The method effectively detected edges by identifying zero-crossings in the Laplacian-filtered, smoothed image. The experiment demonstrated how combining Gaussian noise reduction with Laplacian edge detection provides a balanced approach between sensitivity and noise suppression, enhancing understanding of second-order edge detection techniques _, thresh = cv2.threshold(log_edges, 20, 255, cv2.THRESH_BINARY) plt.figure(figsize=(10,5)) plt.subplot(1,3,1) plt.title("Original") plt.imshow(img, cmap='gray') plt.axis('off') plt.subplot(1,3,2) plt.title("LoG Edge Detection") plt.imshow(log_edges, cmap='gray') plt.axis('off') plt.subplot(1,3,3) plt.title("Thresholded LoG") plt.imshow(thresh, cmap='gray') plt.axis('off') plt.tight_layout() plt.show() Observation, Errors & Learning Write your observations, mention the challenges you faced during performance of this experiment. Also, mention the key learnings that you took from this experiment. Observation (LoG): The LoG result showed thin, well-localized edges. The raw Laplacian output contains both positive and negative values, and the edges are precisely located at the zero-crossings. Taking the absolute value and thresholding highlights the magnitude of change. Observation (DoG): The DoG result visually approximated the LoG result, confirming that subtracting two Gaussians with different $\sigma$ values is an efficient approximation of the Laplacian convolution. Challenge/Errors: For LoG, using the appropriate data type (like cv2.CV_64F or cv2.CV_16S) for the Laplacian output is crucial, as the result contains negative values which would be clipped if a standard uint8 type were used directly. For DoG, the choice of the two standard deviations ($\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$) heavily influences the sensitivity to different scales of edges. Learning: I learned that both LoG and DoG are band-pass filters that suppress low-frequency (smooth areas) and high-frequency (noise) content, making them excellent edge detectors. DoG is computationally faster than applying the LoG convolution kernel directly, making it highly valuable for real-time applications and forming the basis for scale-space feature detection (like SIFT). Conclusion Conclusion of the experiment in your words. This experiment successfully implemented and demonstrated the principles of Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG) and Difference of Gaussians (DoG) edge detection. Both methods effectively localized edges by identifying intensity zero-crossings after noise reduction, with the DoG method proving to be a highly efficient, computational approximation of the theoretically more complex LoG operator. Experiment 6: Title: Image Color Quantization Problem Statement: Write the given problem statement / Aim here - To implement a basic uniform color quantization technique to reduce the number of discrete intensity levels (color depth) in a color image, and analyze the resulting visual distortion. Key Concepts: Names of Key concepts you think you learnt in this experiment Color Quantization: The process of reducing the number of distinct colors used in an image, often to save memory or meet display limitations. Color Depth Reduction: Reducing the number of bits used to represent each color channel (e.g., from 8 bits/channel/256 levels to 5 bits/channel/32 levels). Uniform Quantization: A simple method where the entire range of color values is divided into equal, fixed-size bins. Color Banding/False Contours: The undesirable artifact that appears as visible strips or bands where continuous color gradients are replaced by distinct, quantized color steps. Software / Hardware Setup: List of Software & PC Specs - One: Python 3.x Two: Libraries: NumPy, Pillow (PIL), Matplotlib Three: Operating System and PC Specs Code / Screenshot of Software work (whichever applicable): Python from PIL import Image import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import os script_dir = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath( file )) img = Image.open(os.path.join(script_dir, "iiitn logo.png")).convert("RGB") img_array = np.array(img) print("Original shape:", img_array.shape) quantized_array = ((img_array//8)*8).astype(np.uint8) print("Quantized shape:", quantized_array.shape) plt.figure(figsize=(12,6)) plt.subplot(1, 2, 1) plt.title("Original Image") plt.imshow(img_array) plt.axis("off") plt.subplot(1, 2, 2) plt.title("Quantized Image") plt.imshow(quantized_array) plt.axis("off") plt.show() Output: Original shape: (H, W, 3) Quantized shape: (H, W, 3) Observation, Errors & Learning: Write your observations, mention the challenges you faced during performance of this experiment. Also, mention the key learnings that you took from this experiment. Observation: The quantized image retains its general structure but exhibits a visible reduction in smooth color transitions, particularly in areas with subtle gradients. This is known as color banding. The operation (img_array//8)*8 reduces the 256 possible intensity levels per channel to $256/8 = 32$ levels. Challenge/Errors: Ensuring the arithmetic operations are performed correctly and the result is cast back to np.uint8 is necessary to maintain the image format (0-255 range). The main challenge is observing and characterizing the visual loss of information. Learning: I learned that quantization simplifies color representation by mapping a large range of values to a smaller, discrete set. While necessary for compression, this process results in a loss of data and introduces noticeable banding artifacts where the human visual system can perceive the abrupt jumps between colors. Conclusion: Conclusion of the experiment in your words. The experiment successfully implemented uniform color quantization to reduce the effective color depth of the image. The reduction of intensity levels, achieved by rounding pixel values to the nearest multiple of 8, demonstrated the principle of data compression in the color domain. However, this compression came at the cost of visual fidelity, clearly showing color banding, which is a significant side effect of aggressive quantization.
