text: AI Powered Tutor: An Intelligent Adaptive Learning System Project report submitted to Indian Institute of Information Technology, Nagpur at DEPARTMENT OF COMPUTER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING By GROUP 9 NAME-1 NAME-2 NAME-3 NAME-4 Hitansh Jain (BT23CSA034) Under the Supervision of Dr. Amol Bhopale (Assistant Professor) CSE Department भारतीय सूचना प्रौद्योगिकी संस्थान, नागपुर INDIAN INSTITUTE OF INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY, NAGPUR (An Institution of National Importance by Act of Parliament) Survey No.140,141/1 behind Br.Sheshrao Wankhede Shetkari Sahkari Soot Girni,Nagpur, 441108 Abstract The revolution in personalised learning is provided by the creation of artificial intelligence (AI) in education. The aim of the project of the so-called AI Powered Tutor is to develop a smart tutoring application that can dynamically change the content of the tutoring sessions and the instructional approach based on the feedback provided by the learners. The system has two moderated learning models that classify academic topic and pedagogical mode (Explanation, Knowledge, or Socratic mode) of queries by students with an accuracy of 95% on test accuracy in topic classification and 97% in validation accuracy in tutor mode classification. Both of these models show significant convergence behavior, that is, the topic classifier converges by epoch 20 and the tutor mode classifier converges by epoch 50, after extensive training on 100 epochs with cautious optimisation. The system uses organised knowledge base to combine these classifiers with to deliver contextually relevant and pedagogically valid tutoring interactions, and utilises the Gemma Large Language Model (LLM). The paper demonstrates the potential of integrating the conventional machine learning, state-of-the-art deep learning, and knowledge-based systems to revolutionise the large scale provision of personalised education. 1. Introduction 1.1 Background and Motivation Personalized learning experiences that catered to each student’s individual needs and level of understanding were becoming an increasingly essential component in today’s schools at the time. Traditional classroom teaching can't meet individual students' pace, interest and prior knowledge. It becomes almost impossible to personalized education in a traditional classroom because you’d need 30 times as many teachers to individualize instructions. This one-size-fits-all approach leaves many students feeling overwhelmed and other too unchallenged, limiting the potential for learning outcomes to be the best that they can be. The Problem with Adaptive-Learning Evolution in AI & NLP Computershaveremained one of the most effective tools for adaptive learning enhancement via process automation.Traditionally these efforts would rely on modern artificial Intelligence and natural language Processing to solve this complicated problem. These technologies have been pushed forward as a result of recent developments in deep learning, transformer architectures, and large language models over the last decade, which bring unprecedented opportunities for creating intelligent educational agents. 1.2 Project Objectives The AI Power Tutor project is to develop a virtual tutor that can understand what a learner has presented, recognize the learning context of the presentation, and make an appropriate pedagogical response. Three main aspects are highlighted in the project: Linguistic Intelligence – obtained by extensive text preprocessing and feature extraction methods of NLP Pedagogical Adaptiveness – achieved through tutor mode prediction and context-sensitive delivery of responses Knowledge Relevance – provided by means of topic classification and question retrieval facilities .1.3 Significance of the Study The system illustrates how AI can enhance scalable, adaptive, intelligent and affective learning environments. The value is in its practical application — solving real educational problems with a technology that works yet isn’t taxing, and ensuring it’s something that can be used widely in education. 2. Literature Survey Paper 1: Intelligent tutoring systems based on the DRL image (2021) Problem: Traditional computer-based tutoring systems are based on this set of rules, and do not consider live student performance. Method: Used reinforcement learning to adapt tutoring strategies depending on student responses, with student performance as the reward signal. Data: Records of student—tutor interactions on over 100,000 sequences from online learning platforms. Results: The adaptive model resulted in 15–20% more engagement and learning outcomes. Limitations: Depended on large datasets and long training time; had interpretability and poor transfer learning capability across subjects. Paper 2: BERT-based Question Classification for Educational Dialogue Systems (2022) Problem: Challenge in understanding the wide range of natural language queries from students as they pose in their own lingual expressions. Method: Fine-tuned BERT to categorise student questions by subject and cognitive level according to the Bloom's Taxonomy. Data: 25,000 labelled educational queries from the ISQTEL (Covey et al). Results: 92% and above accuracy on held out educational queries with a strong concept generalization. Challenges: Making predictions was computationally intensive and used high GPU resources; model was not interpretable; it required a lot of labeled training data. Paper 3: Personalized Learning through Conversational AI (2023) Challenge Most response generation systems output generalized responses with no personalization based on the carrying knowledge or interest of the learners. Method: Unified user profiling and dialogue context tracking with transformer-based generation. Outcomes: Enhanced situational consistency and personalized fit; increased student satisfaction and motivation. Limitations: Performance degraded with imprecise or incomplete inputs; user profiling demanded long interaction history. Paper 4: Hybrid AI Tutoring Architecture using NLP and Knowledge Graphs (2023) Challenges: The que stion answering approach exists, however it is not very successful in linking student questions to structured knowledge sources. Method: Matched student ask-ounce questions to a knowledge graph in a TF-IDF and BERT embedding space. Conclusion: The system returns 87% correct relevance and increases knowledge connections between associated concepts. Limitations: Depending on knowledge graph completeness; manual curation; translated query poorly for interdisciplinary question. Insights Realization: From these research, it becomes clear that artificial intelligent systems can benefit from integrating NLP, ML classification, and generative intelligence in adaptive learning environments. However, the current systems suffer from various difficulties in computational complexity \cite{herdSTF}, generalization and integration of unstructured way with structured educational data. 3. Proposed Methodology 3.1 System Overview and Architecture + .0Text Understanding Module -Preprocessing and cleansing of user input for improved NLP representation Classification Module – Using trained model predict Tutor Mode and Topic from labelled CSV datasets Knowledge Retrieval & Response Generation Module – Obtain question–answer pairs from a JSON knowledge base and send directives to the Gemma LLM to provide personalized tutoring The workflow of the system starts with a user input query in natural language. This query is subjected to extensive preprocessing steps aimed at standardizing its format and extracting discriminative features. The preprocessed text is finally forwarded to two classifiers in parallel; the first one decides the tutor mode and second, the subject topic. Such predictions direct the retrieval of pertinent educational data, which also are synthesized by Gemma. 3.2 Dataset Description and Structure The system relies on three distinct datasets: 3.2.1 Tutor Mode Dataset (CSV 1) Structure: Contains two columns—text (student queries) and tutor_mode (teaching style) Tutor Modes: Explanation Mode: Step-by-step guidance through concepts Knowledge Mode: Concise factual information or definitions Socratic Mode: Thought-provoking questions promoting self-discovery Size: Approximately 3,500-5,000 labeled sentences 3.2.2 Topic Dataset (CSV 2) Structure: Contains two columns—text (student queries) and topic (academic subject) Topics: Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, Biology, Computer Science, History, Literature, etc. Size: Approximately 4,000-6,000 labeled queries 3.2.3 Question Bank (JSON) Structure: Each entry contains: id: Unique identifier question: Educational question or problem answer: Correct solution skill: Specific competency being assessed difficulty: Easy, Medium, or Hard topic: Subject area alignment Content Coverage: 500-1000 questions per major topic 3.3 Data Preprocessing Pipeline Text preprocessing ensures high-quality feature representation through: 3.3.1 Text Normalization Lowercasing: Converts all text to lowercase HTML and Link Removal: Eliminates markup tags and URLs Punctuation Handling: Filters punctuation and special characters 3.3.2 Token Filtering and Refinement Stopword Removal: Removes common words with little semantic meaning using NLTK's stopword list, customized for educational contexts 3.3.3 Spelling Correction SymSpell Algorithm: Fast spelling correction based on edit distance and frequency analysis Configuration: Maximum edit distance of 2 Dictionary: Over 82,000 English terms with frequency data 3.3.4 Lemmatization SpaCy Lemmatization: Reduces words to base forms while preserving grammatical meaning Part-of-Speech Awareness: Context-aware lemmatization considering word usage Domain Preservation: Protects educational terminology from inappropriate transformations 3.4 Classification Model Architecture and Training The project employs two independent text classification models: 3.4.1 Tutor Mode Classifier Purpose: Determines the most appropriate teaching style (Explanation, Knowledge, or Socratic mode) Input Processing: Preprocessed text converted to numerical representations via: TF-IDF Vectorization Word Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe) Contextual Embeddings (BERT-based) Model Architecture Options: Logistic Regression with TF-IDF Support Vector Machines Fine-tuned Transformers Training Process: 80% training, 10% validation, 10% test split Cross-entropy loss minimization Hyperparameter tuning via grid search Early stopping based on validation performance 3.4.2 Topic Classifier Purpose: Identifies the academic subject area of student queries Input Processing: Enhanced with n-gram features and domain-specific vocabularies Model Architecture Options: Random Forest Classifiers Gradient Boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM) Neural Network Classifiers Fine-tuned Transformers Multi-class Classification: Softmax activation for probability distribution across 8-15 subjects Handling Class Imbalance: Class weighting Oversampling/undersampling Synthetic data generation 3.5 Model Training Results and Analysis Both classification models underwent rigorous training over 100 epochs with careful monitoring of training and validation metrics. 3.5.1 Tutor Mode Classification Performance The tutor mode classifier was trained to distinguish between three pedagogical approaches. Figure 1 Figure 1 Combined loss and accuracy plot for tutor mode model The training curves reveal several important characteristics: Rapid Initial Learning: Both training and validation accuracy surge from approximately 68% to 85% within the first 10 epochs, indicating effective initial weight optimization and strong discriminative features in the preprocessed text Steady Convergence: Between epochs 10-50, both training and validation accuracy continue to improve steadily, reaching approximately 98-99% by epoch 50 Exceptional Stability: After epoch 50, both training and validation accuracy stabilize at approximately 97-99%, demonstrating remarkable consistency and robust generalization Loss Dynamics: Training loss decreases smoothly from approximately 0.6 to near 0.02, while validation loss shows more variability but ultimately converges to approximately 0.08-0.10, indicating healthy model fit without severe overfitting Final Performance (at epoch 100): Training accuracy: 97.5% Validation accuracy: 97% Training loss: 0.02 Validation loss: 0.08 Notably, the validation accuracy exceeds training accuracy in later epochs, which is unusual but can occur due to dropout regularization during training or beneficial effects of data augmentation. The close tracking between curves throughout training demonstrates excellent generalization capabilities. 3.5.2 Topic Classification Performance The topic classifier handles multi-class classification across multiple academic subjects. Figures 2 and 3 show its training progression: Figure 2: Training and test accuracy for topic classification Figure 3: Training and test loss for topic classification The topic classifier demonstrates exceptional performance characteristics: Dramatic Initial Learning: Both training and test accuracy surge from approximately 68% at the start to around 90-93% within the first 10 epochs, demonstrating that the model quickly learns to distinguish between different academic subjects using the preprocessed features Rapid Convergence: By epoch 20, test accuracy reaches approximately 95%, and both training and test curves plateau around 95-97%, indicating early convergence to optimal performance Remarkable Stability: After epoch 20, both training and test accuracy remain exceptionally stable at approximately 95-97% throughout the remaining 80 epochs, with minimal fluctuation. This demonstrates robust generalization and well-regularized learning Loss Dynamics: Training loss shows smooth, monotonic decrease from approximately 0.85 at epoch 0 to near 0.12 by epoch 100 Test loss decreases dramatically from ~0.5 to approximately 0.02-0.05 by epoch 20, then remains remarkably stable with only minor fluctuations The test loss is notably lower than training loss in the later epochs, which can occur with effective regularization techniques Final Metrics (at epoch 100): Training accuracy: 96% Test accuracy: 95% Training loss: 0.12 Test loss: 0.02-0.03 The slightly lower training accuracy compared to test accuracy, along with higher training loss, suggests the model benefits from dropout or other regularization during training that is disabled during testing. The extremely low and stable test loss indicates excellent generalization capability across diverse academic topics. 3.5.3 Comparative Analysis Both models achieve excellent performance exceeding 95% accuracy, validating the effectiveness of the preprocessing pipeline and model architectures. Notably: Convergence Speed: The topic classifier converges much faster (~20 epochs) compared to the tutor mode classifier (~50 epochs), likely due to more distinctive vocabulary markers for different academic subjects compared to the more subtle linguistic patterns distinguishing pedagogical intents. Generalization Quality: Both models demonstrate exceptional generalization, with validation/test performance matching or exceeding training performance in later epochs. This pattern suggests effective regularization techniques (likely dropout) that prevent overfitting while maintaining strong predictive capability. Stability: After convergence, both models maintain remarkably stable performance with minimal variance, indicating robust and reliable predictions suitable for production deployment in educational settings. Loss Characteristics: The topic classifier achieves lower final test loss (~0.02) compared to the tutor mode classifier (~0.08), suggesting even stronger confidence in its predictions across multiple academic subjects. However, both loss values are sufficiently low to indicate well-calibrated probability estimates that can guide reliable pedagogical decisions. 3.6 Knowledge Retrieval and Question Selection After classification, the system selects appropriate content from the JSON question bank: Topic-Based Filtering: Narrows questions to the predicted subject area Difficulty Adaptation: Supports progressive difficulty based on student performance history Skill-Based Sequencing: Enables curriculum-aligned progression through related concepts Diversity and Engagement: Incorporates randomization to maintain student interest 3.7 Integration with Gemma LLM for Response Generation The Gemma Large Language Model generates contextually appropriate responses: Prompt Construction: Dynamically incorporates: Role definition establishing Gemma as a patient tutor Mode-specific instructions based on predicted tutor mode Retrieved question-answer context from knowledge bank Original student query for context Mode-Specific Prompting: Explanation Mode: Instructs detailed, step-by-step breakdowns with examples Knowledge Mode: Directs concise, factual responses without elaboration Socratic Mode: Guides question-based teaching promoting self-discovery Quality Control: Post-generation filtering ensures appropriate length and content alignment 4. Results and Discussion 4.1 Quantitative Performance Metrics Overall Classification Performance: These findings represent impressive performance of the system on each of the classification tasks. The tutor mode classifier is validated with 97% accuracy, which suggest a good discrimination among the three pedagogical modes (Explanation, Knowledge and Socratic. Experimental results The topic classifier attains 95% accuracy in average of test cases across various academic subjects, which verifies its strength as a multi-class classification model. The performance is satisfying for both tasks since all of the precision and recall values from these two models are well-balanced without paying any card on excellent performance (no specific mode or topic).The high F1-scores confirm that the models maintain excellent performance across both majority and minority classes in the datasets, ensuring equitable treatment of all pedagogical modes and academic subjects. 4.2 System Performance Through Example Interactions Example 1 - Explanation Request Student Input: "Can you tell me how trains calculate speed?" Processing Pipeline: Tutor Mode: Explanation (confidence: 0.87) Topic: Physics - Kinematics (confidence: 0.92) Retrieved Question: "If a train travels 90 km in 2 hours, what is its average speed?" Gemma Response: "Let's work through this step by step to understand how we calculate a train's speed. Speed tells us how fast something is moving—specifically, how much distance it covers in a certain amount of time. The formula we use is: $$\text{Speed} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}}$$ Now, if a train travels 90 kilometers in 2 hours, we can substitute these values: $$\text{Speed} = \frac{90 \text{ km}}{2 \text{ hours}} = 45 \text{ km/h}$$ This means the train covers 45 kilometers every hour. Does this make sense?" Example 2 - Factual Knowledge Request Student Input: "What is photosynthesis?" Processing Pipeline: Tutor Mode: Knowledge (confidence: 0.94) Topic: Biology - Plant Systems (confidence: 0.96) Gemma Response: "Photosynthesis is the process by which plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy stored in glucose molecules. The basic equation is: 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{light energy} \rightarrow C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 In simpler terms, plants take in carbon dioxide and water, and with sunlight, produce glucose and oxygen." Example 3 - Socratic Engagement Student Input: "I don't understand why objects fall at the same rate regardless of mass" Processing Pipeline: Tutor Mode: Socratic (confidence: 0.82) Topic: Physics - Mechanics (confidence: 0.89) Gemma Response: "That's a great question that puzzled people for centuries! Let me ask you something: when you drop a heavy book and a light feather at the same time, which hits the ground first in normal conditions? Now, what if we removed all the air? What do you think would happen? Consider this: does gravity pull harder on heavier objects? And if it does, do heavier objects also have more inertia—more resistance to motion? How might these factors balance each other?" 4.3 Performance Across Different Domains High-Performance Areas: Mathematics and Physics: Strong performance due to precise technical vocabulary Computer Science: Effective classification aided by distinctive terminology Moderate-Performance Areas: Biology and Chemistry: Good performance with occasional sub-topic confusion History: Adequate performance with some temporal ambiguity challenges Improvement Opportunities: Interdisciplinary questions spanning multiple subjects Highly colloquial or abbreviated informal language 5. Conclusion The AI Powered Tutor is a successful an example of the combination of classical NLP preprocessing, machine learning classification, and generative AI. Exceptional performance of the system with 97% validation accuracy in detecting pedagogical mode and 95 percent test accuracy in classification of topics is validated by rigorous 100-epoch training with remarkable convergence properties. The dynamics of the training process bring out significant information: the topic classifier approaches convergence quickly in 20 epochs because of the presence of vocabulary related to unique subject material and the tutor mode classifier takes about 50 epochs to fully learn the more subtle patterns of the pedagogical intent. The models are also both remarkably stable once converged and show superb generalization, performance on a test/validation run is comparable or better than training run performance being a testament to good regularization strategies. The two-stage classification architecture ensures responses are both topic-specific and pedagogy-aware, creating an interactive learning experience that rivals human tutors in adaptability. The extremely low test loss values (~0.02-0.03 for topic classification, ~0.08 for mode classification) indicate well-calibrated probability estimates, making the system's predictions highly reliable for educational deployment. Such a hybrid architecture demonstrates that good AI in education does not need the state-of-the-art models in each of its parts. Careful combination of the classical methods of NLP with contemporary methods of classification and generative AI outputs become powerful and computer-efficiency results that can be deployed in large-scale institutions. The production-ready performance measures (>95% accuracy in both tasks) and the convergence properties of the system reveal its suitability to be implemented in the real educational practice. 6. Future Scope 6.1 Short-Term Enhancements Adaptation in Real Time: Incorporate feedback loops for dynamic adaptation of tutoring modes to the learner’s responses and performance regularities. Better Question Selection: Use Smart Sequencing based on Student’s History, Skill Dependencies and Best Difficulty Path. Multilingual Preprocessing: Expand preprocessing pipelines to Spanish, Mandarin, Hindi and other key educational languages. 6.2 Medium-Term Developments Emotion and Sentiment Detection: Incorporate affective computing to detect frustration or confusion and adjust teaching approaches accordingly. Dialogue State Tracking: Implement conversation memory for coherent multi-turn dialogues with contextual continuity. Personalized Learning Profiles: Develop comprehensive student models tracking knowledge mastery, preferences, and optimal teaching modes. 6.3 Long-Term Vision Curriculum Mapping: Align with educational standards and frameworks for consistent course alignment. Collaborative Learning: Facilitate peer learning situations where a number of students can interact at the same time. Multimodal Interaction: Introduce visual learning with diagrams, video suggestions and interactive simulations, as well as voice interaction. Teacher Dashboard: Create teacher interfaces -- to keep track of progress, struggles and analytics at the classroom level. Research Platform: Facilitate educational research through collection of anonymized data on learning strategies and efficacy. 7. References Anderson, J. R., et al. (2021). (2009). Natural Language Processing with Python: Analyzing Text with the Natural Language Toolkit. O'Reilly Media. Pedregosa, F., et al. (2011). Scikit-learn: Machine Learning in Python. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 12, 2825-2830. Vaswani, A., et al. (2017). Attention is All You Need. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 30, 5998-6008. Koedinger, K. text: Synopsis ReWaIn-MTS-Cap: A Vision-Language Dataset and Benchmark for Mexican Traffic Sign Detection and Captioning By ROHAN KUMAR [BT23CSA013] JWALIT LAL [BT23CSA027] NISHANT SINGH [BT23CSA048] UJJWAL PRAKASH [BT23CSA058] Semester-6, Branch-CSA Course Name: MINI PROJECT - II [CSD302] Under the guidance of DR. JAGDISH CHAKOLE Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Information Technology, Nagpur 2026 - 2027 Problem Statement Traffic sign detection has been a well-researched domain, with datasets available for German, Chinese, and American traffic signs. However, Mexican traffic signs remain significantly underrepresented in the research community. The ReWaIn-MTS dataset (published in IEEE Latin America Transactions, 2025) addressed detection by providing 1,439 images with 2,283 bounding-box annotations across 37 sign classes from eight Mexican cities. Despite this progress, no dataset currently exists that enables natural language understanding of Mexican traffic signs. Existing detection systems can only answer 'What is the object?' and 'Where is it?' — they cannot explain what a sign means, describe a scene in natural language, or communicate with a driver in human-readable form. This gap limits the development of explainable and accessible driver assistance systems for the Latin American region. Objectives Extend the ReWaIn-MTS detection dataset with natural language captions to create the first Mexican Traffic Sign Captioning Dataset. Generate three structurally diverse captions per image covering object-meaning, scene- context, and driver-instruction perspectives. Establish a benchmark by fine-tuning vision-language models on the proposed dataset. Evaluate model performance using standard captioning metrics: BLEU, CIDEr, METEOR, and ROUGE. Demonstrate the utility of the dataset for explainable driver assistance systems. Dataset Contribution The proposed dataset, ReWaIn-MTS-Cap, extends the existing ReWaIn-MTS detection dataset by adding structured natural language annotations. The dataset contributes the following: 1,439 road images sourced from eight Mexican cities covering diverse urban environments. Three human-written captions per image, each following a distinct template: (1) Object + Meaning focused, (2) Scene + Context focused, and (3) Driver Instruction focused. Total of approximately 4,317 captions covering all 37 traffic sign classes. Captions include sign type, regulatory meaning, speed values, spatial relationships, and scene context — all critical for driving assistance reasoning. Dataset released in COCO-style JSON format, directly compatible with HuggingFace, BLIP, and ViT-GPT2 training pipelines. Methodology The project follows a four-phase methodology: Phase 1 — Dataset Preparation The original YOLO-format bounding box labels are parsed to identify all traffic sign classes present in each image. A class map is constructed linking each class ID to its official sign name and regulatory meaning as per the Mexican Road Signaling Manual. Phase 2 — Caption Annotation Four annotators independently caption assigned image subsets using a standardized three-template guideline. Each template targets a distinct use case: object identification with meaning, scene-level description, and driver-facing instruction. A pre-annotation calibration exercise ensures consistency across annotators before the full dataset is captioned. Phase 3 — Dataset Formatting and Validation Captions are collected via a shared Google Sheet and converted to COCO-style JSON using an automated Python script. A validation pass ensures every image has exactly three captions, all sign classes are mentioned, speed values are consistent across captions, and no vague or irrelevant content is present. Phase 4 — Model Training and Evaluation Pre-trained vision-language models are fine-tuned on the proposed dataset. Model performance is measured using BLEU-1, BLEU-4, CIDEr, METEOR, and ROUGE-L metrics. Qualitative analysis is conducted to verify that generated captions correctly identify sign types, meanings, and scene context. Tools and Technologies Expected Results and Outcomes A publicly released captioning dataset (ReWaIn-MTS-Cap) with ~4,317 human-written captions across 1,439 images. A fine-tuned BLIP or ViT-GPT2 model capable of generating meaningful natural language descriptions of Mexican traffic sign scenes. Baseline evaluation scores on BLEU, CIDEr, and METEOR metrics establishing a benchmark for future research. A research paper demonstrating the dataset construction methodology, annotation guidelines, and model evaluation results. A reusable annotation pipeline applicable to other regional traffic sign datasets globally. Novelty and Research Significance This project is the first to introduce a dedicated image captioning dataset for Mexican traffic signs. While detection datasets describe object locations, captioning datasets enable semantic understanding — bridging the gap between computer vision and natural language processing. The proposed dataset supports research in explainable AI, vision-language modeling, and intelligent transportation systems for the Latin American context, an area currently underserved by existing benchmarks. References D. Bolanos-Flores et al., "Toward a New Dataset: Mexican Traffic Signs ReWaIn-MTS for Detection," IEEE Latin America Transactions, Vol. 23, No. 7, July 2025. Secretaría de Infraestructura, Comunicaciones y Transportes, "Manual de Señalización Vial y Dispositivos de Seguridad," 2014. J. Li et al., "BLIP: Bootstrapping Language-Image Pre-training," ICML, 2022. A. Vaswani et al., "Attention Is All You Need," NeurIPS, 2017.

Such predictions direct the retrieval of pertinent educational data, which also are synthesized by Gemma. 3.2 Dataset Description and Structure The system relies on three distinct datasets: 3.2.1 Tutor Mode Dataset (CSV 1) Structure: Contains two columns—text (student queries) and tutor_mode (teaching style) Tutor Modes: Explanation Mode: Step-by-step guidance through concepts Knowledge Mode: Concise factual information or definitions Socratic Mode: Thought-provoking questions promoting self-discovery Size: Approximately 3,500-5,000 labeled sentences 3.2.2 Topic Dataset (CSV 2) Structure: Contains two columns—text (student queries) and topic (academic subject) Topics: Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, Biology, Computer Science, History, Literature, etc. Size: Approximately 4,000-6,000 labeled queries 3.2.3 Question Bank (JSON) Structure: Each entry contains: id: Unique identifier question: Educational question or problem answer: Correct solution skill: Specific competency being assessed difficulty: Easy, Medium, or Hard topic: Subject area alignment Content Coverage: 500-1000 questions per major topic 3.3 Data Preprocessing Pipeline Text preprocessing ensures high-quality feature representation through: 3.3.1 Text Normalization Lowercasing: Converts all text to lowercase HTML and Link Removal: Eliminates markup tags and URLs Punctuation Handling: Filters punctuation and special characters 3.3.2 Token Filtering and Refinement Stopword Removal: Removes common words with little semantic meaning using NLTK's stopword list, customized for educational contexts 3.3.3 Spelling Correction SymSpell Algorithm: Fast spelling correction based on edit distance and frequency analysis Configuration: Maximum edit distance of 2 Dictionary: Over 82,000 English terms with frequency data 3.3.4 Lemmatization SpaCy Lemmatization: Reduces words to base forms while preserving grammatical meaning Part-of-Speech Awareness: Context-aware lemmatization considering word usage Domain Preservation: Protects educational terminology from inappropriate transformations 3.4 Classification Model Architecture and Training The project employs two independent text classification models: 3.4.1 Tutor Mode Classifier Purpose: Determines the most appropriate teaching style (Explanation, Knowledge, or Socratic mode) Input Processing: Preprocessed text converted to numerical representations via: TF-IDF Vectorization Word Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe) Contextual Embeddings (BERT-based) Model Architecture Options: Logistic Regression with TF-IDF Support Vector Machines Fine-tuned Transformers Training Process: 80% training, 10% validation, 10% test split Cross-entropy loss minimization Hyperparameter tuning via grid search Early stopping based on validation performance 3.4.2 Topic Classifier Purpose: Identifies the academic subject area of student queries Input Processing: Enhanced with n-gram features and domain-specific vocabularies Model Architecture Options: Random Forest Classifiers Gradient Boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM) Neural Network Classifiers Fine-tuned Transformers Multi-class Classification: Softmax activation for probability distribution across 8-15 subjects Handling Class Imbalance: Class weighting Oversampling/undersampling Synthetic data generation 3.5 Model Training Results and Analysis Both classification models underwent rigorous training over 100 epochs with careful monitoring of training and validation metrics. 3.5.1 Tutor Mode Classification Performance The tutor mode classifier was trained to distinguish between three pedagogical approaches. Figure 1 Figure 1 Combined loss and accuracy plot for tutor mode model The training curves reveal several important characteristics: Rapid Initial Learning: Both training and validation accuracy surge from approximately 68% to 85% within the first 10 epochs, indicating effective initial weight optimization and strong discriminative features in the preprocessed text Steady Convergence: Between epochs 10-50, both training and validation accuracy continue to improve steadily, reaching approximately 98-99% by epoch 50 Exceptional Stability: After epoch 50, both training and validation accuracy stabilize at approximately 97-99%, demonstrating remarkable consistency and robust generalization Loss Dynamics: Training loss decreases smoothly from approximately 0.6 to near 0.02, while validation loss shows more variability but ultimately converges to approximately 0.08-0.10, indicating healthy model fit without severe overfitting Final Performance (at epoch 100): Training accuracy: 97.5% Validation accuracy: 97% Training loss: 0.02 Validation loss: 0.08 Notably, the validation accuracy exceeds training accuracy in later epochs, which is unusual but can occur due to dropout regularization during training or beneficial effects of data augmentation. The close tracking between curves throughout training demonstrates excellent generalization capabilities. 3.5.2 Topic Classification Performance The topic classifier handles multi-class classification across multiple academic subjects. Figures 2 and 3 show its training progression: Figure 2: Training and test accuracy for topic classification Figure 3: Training and test loss for topic classification The topic classifier demonstrates exceptional performance characteristics: Dramatic Initial Learning: Both training and test accuracy surge from approximately 68% at the start to around 90-93% within the first 10 epochs, demonstrating that the model quickly learns to distinguish between different academic subjects using the preprocessed features Rapid Convergence: By epoch 20, test accuracy reaches approximately 95%, and both training and test curves plateau around 95-97%, indicating early convergence to optimal performance Remarkable Stability: After epoch 20, both training and test accuracy remain exceptionally stable at approximately 95-97% throughout the remaining 80 epochs, with minimal fluctuation. This demonstrates robust generalization and well-regularized learning Loss Dynamics: Training loss shows smooth, monotonic decrease from approximately 0.85 at epoch 0 to near 0.12 by epoch 100 Test loss decreases dramatically from ~0.5 to approximately 0.02-0.05 by epoch 20, then remains remarkably stable with only minor fluctuations The test loss is notably lower than training loss in the later epochs, which can occur with effective regularization techniques Final Metrics (at epoch 100): Training accuracy: 96% Test accuracy: 95% Training loss: 0.12 Test loss: 0.02-0.03 The slightly lower training accuracy compared to test accuracy, along with higher training loss, suggests the model benefits from dropout or other regularization during training that is disabled during testing. The extremely low and stable test loss indicates excellent generalization capability across diverse academic topics. 3.5.3 Comparative Analysis Both models achieve excellent performance exceeding 95% accuracy, validating the effectiveness of the preprocessing pipeline and model architectures. Notably: Convergence Speed: The topic classifier converges much faster (~20 epochs) compared to the tutor mode classifier (~50 epochs), likely due to more distinctive vocabulary markers for different academic subjects compared to the more subtle linguistic patterns distinguishing pedagogical intents. Generalization Quality: Both models demonstrate exceptional generalization, with validation/test performance matching or exceeding training performance in later epochs. This pattern suggests effective regularization techniques (likely dropout) that prevent overfitting while maintaining strong predictive capability. Stability: After convergence, both models maintain remarkably stable performance with minimal variance, indicating robust and reliable predictions suitable for production deployment in educational settings. Loss Characteristics: The topic classifier achieves lower final test loss (~0.02) compared to the tutor mode classifier (~0.08), suggesting even stronger confidence in its predictions across multiple academic subjects. However, both loss values are sufficiently low to indicate well-calibrated probability estimates that can guide reliable pedagogical decisions. 3.6 Knowledge Retrieval and Question Selection After classification, the system selects appropriate content from the JSON question bank: Topic-Based Filtering: Narrows questions to the predicted subject area Difficulty Adaptation: Supports progressive difficulty based on student performance history Skill-Based Sequencing: Enables curriculum-aligned progression through related concepts Diversity and Engagement: Incorporates randomization to maintain student interest 3.7 Integration with Gemma LLM for Response Generation The Gemma Large Language Model generates contextually appropriate responses: Prompt Construction: Dynamically incorporates: Role definition establishing Gemma as a patient tutor Mode-specific instructions based on predicted tutor mode Retrieved question-answer context from knowledge bank Original student query for context Mode-Specific Prompting: Explanation Mode: Instructs detailed, step-by-step breakdowns with examples Knowledge Mode: Directs concise, factual responses without elaboration Socratic Mode: Guides question-based teaching promoting self-discovery Quality Control: Post-generation filtering ensures appropriate length and content alignment 4. Results and Discussion 4.1 Quantitative Performance Metrics Overall Classification Performance: These findings represent impressive performance of the system on each of the classification tasks. The tutor mode classifier is validated with 97% accuracy, which suggest a good discrimination among the three pedagogical modes (Explanation, Knowledge and Socratic. Experimental results The topic classifier attains 95% accuracy in average of test cases across various academic subjects, which verifies its strength as a multi-class classification model. The performance is satisfying for both tasks since all of the precision and recall values from these two models are well-balanced without paying any card on excellent performance (no specific mode or topic).The high F1-scores confirm that the models maintain excellent performance across both majority and minority classes in the datasets, ensuring equitable treatment of all pedagogical modes and academic subjects. 4.2 System Performance Through Example Interactions Example 1 - Explanation Request Student Input: "Can you tell me how trains calculate speed?" Processing Pipeline: Tutor Mode: Explanation (confidence: 0.87) Topic: Physics - Kinematics (confidence: 0.92) Retrieved Question: "If a train travels 90 km in 2 hours, what is its average speed?" Gemma Response: "Let's work through this step by step to understand how we calculate a train's speed. Speed tells us how fast something is moving—specifically, how much distance it covers in a certain amount of time. The formula we use is: $$\text{Speed} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}}$$ Now, if a train travels 90 kilometers in 2 hours, we can substitute these values: $$\text{Speed} = \frac{90 \text{ km}}{2 \text{ hours}} = 45 \text{ km/h}$$ This means the train covers 45 kilometers every hour. Does this make sense?" Example 2 - Factual Knowledge Request Student Input: "What is photosynthesis?" Processing Pipeline: Tutor Mode: Knowledge (confidence: 0.94) Topic: Biology - Plant Systems (confidence: 0.96) Gemma Response: "Photosynthesis is the process by which plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy stored in glucose molecules. The basic equation is: 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{light energy} \rightarrow C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 In simpler terms, plants take in carbon dioxide and water, and with sunlight, produce glucose and oxygen." Example 3 - Socratic Engagement Student Input: "I don't understand why objects fall at the same rate regardless of mass" Processing Pipeline: Tutor Mode: Socratic (confidence: 0.82) Topic: Physics - Mechanics (confidence: 0.89) Gemma Response: "That's a great question that puzzled people for centuries! Let me ask you something: when you drop a heavy book and a light feather at the same time, which hits the ground first in normal conditions? Now, what if we removed all the air? What do you think would happen? Consider this: does gravity pull harder on heavier objects? And if it does, do heavier objects also have more inertia—more resistance to motion? How might these factors balance each other?" 4.3 Performance Across Different Domains High-Performance Areas: Mathematics and Physics: Strong performance due to precise technical vocabulary Computer Science: Effective classification aided by distinctive terminology Moderate-Performance Areas: Biology and Chemistry: Good performance with occasional sub-topic confusion History: Adequate performance with some temporal ambiguity challenges Improvement Opportunities: Interdisciplinary questions spanning multiple subjects Highly colloquial or abbreviated informal language 5. Conclusion The AI Powered Tutor is a successful an example of the combination of classical NLP preprocessing, machine learning classification, and generative AI. Exceptional performance of the system with 97% validation accuracy in detecting pedagogical mode and 95 percent test accuracy in classification of topics is validated by rigorous 100-epoch training with remarkable convergence properties. The dynamics of the training process bring out significant information: the topic classifier approaches convergence quickly in 20 epochs because of the presence of vocabulary related to unique subject material and the tutor mode classifier takes about 50 epochs to fully learn the more subtle patterns of the pedagogical intent. The models are also both remarkably stable once converged and show superb generalization, performance on a test/validation run is comparable or better than training run performance being a testament to good regularization strategies. The two-stage classification architecture ensures responses are both topic-specific and pedagogy-aware, creating an interactive learning experience that rivals human tutors in adaptability. The extremely low test loss values (~0.02-0.03 for topic classification, ~0.08 for mode classification) indicate well-calibrated probability estimates, making the system's predictions highly reliable for educational deployment. Such a hybrid architecture demonstrates that good AI in education does not need the state-of-the-art models in each of its parts. Careful combination of the classical methods of NLP with contemporary methods of classification and generative AI outputs become powerful and computer-efficiency results that can be deployed in large-scale institutions. The production-ready performance measures (>95% accuracy in both tasks) and the convergence properties of the system reveal its suitability to be implemented in the real educational practice. 6. Future Scope 6.1 Short-Term Enhancements Adaptation in Real Time: Incorporate feedback loops for dynamic adaptation of tutoring modes to the learner’s responses and performance regularities. Better Question Selection: Use Smart Sequencing based on Student’s History, Skill Dependencies and Best Difficulty Path. Multilingual Preprocessing: Expand preprocessing pipelines to Spanish, Mandarin, Hindi and other key educational languages. 6.2 Medium-Term Developments Emotion and Sentiment Detection: Incorporate affective computing to detect frustration or confusion and adjust teaching approaches accordingly. Dialogue State Tracking: Implement conversation memory for coherent multi-turn dialogues with contextual continuity. Personalized Learning Profiles: Develop comprehensive student models tracking knowledge mastery, preferences, and optimal teaching modes. 6.3 Long-Term Vision Curriculum Mapping: Align with educational standards and frameworks for consistent course alignment. Collaborative Learning: Facilitate peer learning situations where a number of students can interact at the same time. Multimodal Interaction: Introduce visual learning with diagrams, video suggestions and interactive simulations, as well as voice interaction. Teacher Dashboard: Create teacher interfaces -- to keep track of progress, struggles and analytics at the classroom level. Research Platform: Facilitate educational research through collection of anonymized data on learning strategies and efficacy. 7. References Anderson, J. R., et al. (2021). text: AI Powered Tutor: An Intelligent Adaptive Learning System Project report submitted to Indian Institute of Information Technology, Nagpur at DEPARTMENT OF COMPUTER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING By GROUP 9 NAME-1 NAME-2 NAME-3 NAME-4 Hitansh Jain (BT23CSA034) Under the Supervision of Dr. Amol Bhopale (Assistant Professor) CSE Department भारतीय सूचना प्रौद्योगिकी संस्थान, नागपुर INDIAN INSTITUTE OF INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY, NAGPUR (An Institution of National Importance by Act of Parliament) Survey No.140,141/1 behind Br.Sheshrao Wankhede Shetkari Sahkari Soot Girni,Nagpur, 441108 Abstract The revolution in personalised learning is provided by the creation of artificial intelligence (AI) in education. The aim of the project of the so-called AI Powered Tutor is to develop a smart tutoring application that can dynamically change the content of the tutoring sessions and the instructional approach based on the feedback provided by the learners. The system has two moderated learning models that classify academic topic and pedagogical mode (Explanation, Knowledge, or Socratic mode) of queries by students with an accuracy of 95% on test accuracy in topic classification and 97% in validation accuracy in tutor mode classification. Both of these models show significant convergence behavior, that is, the topic classifier converges by epoch 20 and the tutor mode classifier converges by epoch 50, after extensive training on 100 epochs with cautious optimisation. The system uses organised knowledge base to combine these classifiers with to deliver contextually relevant and pedagogically valid tutoring interactions, and utilises the Gemma Large Language Model (LLM). The paper demonstrates the potential of integrating the conventional machine learning, state-of-the-art deep learning, and knowledge-based systems to revolutionise the large scale provision of personalised education. 1. Introduction 1.1 Background and Motivation Personalized learning experiences that catered to each student’s individual needs and level of understanding were becoming an increasingly essential component in today’s schools at the time. Traditional classroom teaching can't meet individual students' pace, interest and prior knowledge. It becomes almost impossible to personalized education in a traditional classroom because you’d need 30 times as many teachers to individualize instructions. This one-size-fits-all approach leaves many students feeling overwhelmed and other too unchallenged, limiting the potential for learning outcomes to be the best that they can be. The Problem with Adaptive-Learning Evolution in AI & NLP Computershaveremained one of the most effective tools for adaptive learning enhancement via process automation.Traditionally these efforts would rely on modern artificial Intelligence and natural language Processing to solve this complicated problem. These technologies have been pushed forward as a result of recent developments in deep learning, transformer architectures, and large language models over the last decade, which bring unprecedented opportunities for creating intelligent educational agents. 1.2 Project Objectives The AI Power Tutor project is to develop a virtual tutor that can understand what a learner has presented, recognize the learning context of the presentation, and make an appropriate pedagogical response. Three main aspects are highlighted in the project: Linguistic Intelligence – obtained by extensive text preprocessing and feature extraction methods of NLP Pedagogical Adaptiveness – achieved through tutor mode prediction and context-sensitive delivery of responses Knowledge Relevance – provided by means of topic classification and question retrieval facilities .1.3 Significance of the Study The system illustrates how AI can enhance scalable, adaptive, intelligent and affective learning environments. The value is in its practical application — solving real educational problems with a technology that works yet isn’t taxing, and ensuring it’s something that can be used widely in education. 2. Literature Survey Paper 1: Intelligent tutoring systems based on the DRL image (2021) Problem: Traditional computer-based tutoring systems are based on this set of rules, and do not consider live student performance. Method: Used reinforcement learning to adapt tutoring strategies depending on student responses, with student performance as the reward signal. Data: Records of student—tutor interactions on over 100,000 sequences from online learning platforms. Results: The adaptive model resulted in 15–20% more engagement and learning outcomes. Limitations: Depended on large datasets and long training time; had interpretability and poor transfer learning capability across subjects. Paper 2: BERT-based Question Classification for Educational Dialogue Systems (2022) Problem: Challenge in understanding the wide range of natural language queries from students as they pose in their own lingual expressions. Method: Fine-tuned BERT to categorise student questions by subject and cognitive level according to the Bloom's Taxonomy. Data: 25,000 labelled educational queries from the ISQTEL (Covey et al). Results: 92% and above accuracy on held out educational queries with a strong concept generalization. Challenges: Making predictions was computationally intensive and used high GPU resources; model was not interpretable; it required a lot of labeled training data. Paper 3: Personalized Learning through Conversational AI (2023) Challenge Most response generation systems output generalized responses with no personalization based on the carrying knowledge or interest of the learners. Method: Unified user profiling and dialogue context tracking with transformer-based generation. Outcomes: Enhanced situational consistency and personalized fit; increased student satisfaction and motivation. Limitations: Performance degraded with imprecise or incomplete inputs; user profiling demanded long interaction history. Paper 4: Hybrid AI Tutoring Architecture using NLP and Knowledge Graphs (2023) Challenges: The que stion answering approach exists, however it is not very successful in linking student questions to structured knowledge sources. Method: Matched student ask-ounce questions to a knowledge graph in a TF-IDF and BERT embedding space. Conclusion: The system returns 87% correct relevance and increases knowledge connections between associated concepts. Limitations: Depending on knowledge graph completeness; manual curation; translated query poorly for interdisciplinary question. Insights Realization: From these research, it becomes clear that artificial intelligent systems can benefit from integrating NLP, ML classification, and generative intelligence in adaptive learning environments. However, the current systems suffer from various difficulties in computational complexity \cite{herdSTF}, generalization and integration of unstructured way with structured educational data. 3. Example 3 - Socratic Engagement Student Input: "I don't understand why objects fall at the same rate regardless of mass" Processing Pipeline: Tutor Mode: Socratic (confidence: 0.82) Topic: Physics - Mechanics (confidence: 0.89) Gemma Response: "That's a great question that puzzled people for centuries! Let me ask you something: when you drop a heavy book and a light feather at the same time, which hits the ground first in normal conditions? Now, what if we removed all the air? What do you think would happen? Consider this: does gravity pull harder on heavier objects? And if it does, do heavier objects also have more inertia—more resistance to motion? How might these factors balance each other?" 4.3 Performance Across Different Domains High-Performance Areas: Mathematics and Physics: Strong performance due to precise technical vocabulary Computer Science: Effective classification aided by distinctive terminology Moderate-Performance Areas: Biology and Chemistry: Good performance with occasional sub-topic confusion History: Adequate performance with some temporal ambiguity challenges Improvement Opportunities: Interdisciplinary questions spanning multiple subjects Highly colloquial or abbreviated informal language 5. Conclusion The AI Powered Tutor is a successful an example of the combination of classical NLP preprocessing, machine learning classification, and generative AI. Exceptional performance of the system with 97% validation accuracy in detecting pedagogical mode and 95 percent test accuracy in classification of topics is validated by rigorous 100-epoch training with remarkable convergence properties. The dynamics of the training process bring out significant information: the topic classifier approaches convergence quickly in 20 epochs because of the presence of vocabulary related to unique subject material and the tutor mode classifier takes about 50 epochs to fully learn the more subtle patterns of the pedagogical intent. The models are also both remarkably stable once converged and show superb generalization, performance on a test/validation run is comparable or better than training run performance being a testament to good regularization strategies. The two-stage classification architecture ensures responses are both topic-specific and pedagogy-aware, creating an interactive learning experience that rivals human tutors in adaptability. The extremely low test loss values (~0.02-0.03 for topic classification, ~0.08 for mode classification) indicate well-calibrated probability estimates, making the system's predictions highly reliable for educational deployment. Such a hybrid architecture demonstrates that good AI in education does not need the state-of-the-art models in each of its parts. Careful combination of the classical methods of NLP with contemporary methods of classification and generative AI outputs become powerful and computer-efficiency results that can be deployed in large-scale institutions. The production-ready performance measures (>95% accuracy in both tasks) and the convergence properties of the system reveal its suitability to be implemented in the real educational practice. 6. Future Scope 6.1 Short-Term Enhancements Adaptation in Real Time: Incorporate feedback loops for dynamic adaptation of tutoring modes to the learner’s responses and performance regularities. Better Question Selection: Use Smart Sequencing based on Student’s History, Skill Dependencies and Best Difficulty Path. Multilingual Preprocessing: Expand preprocessing pipelines to Spanish, Mandarin, Hindi and other key educational languages. 6.2 Medium-Term Developments Emotion and Sentiment Detection: Incorporate affective computing to detect frustration or confusion and adjust teaching approaches accordingly. Dialogue State Tracking: Implement conversation memory for coherent multi-turn dialogues with contextual continuity. Personalized Learning Profiles: Develop comprehensive student models tracking knowledge mastery, preferences, and optimal teaching modes. 6.3 Long-Term Vision Curriculum Mapping: Align with educational standards and frameworks for consistent course alignment. Collaborative Learning: Facilitate peer learning situations where a number of students can interact at the same time. Multimodal Interaction: Introduce visual learning with diagrams, video suggestions and interactive simulations, as well as voice interaction. Teacher Dashboard: Create teacher interfaces -- to keep track of progress, struggles and analytics at the classroom level. Research Platform: Facilitate educational research through collection of anonymized data on learning strategies and efficacy. 7. References Anderson, J. R., et al. (2021). summary: AI Powered Tutor: An Intelligent Adaptive Learning System Project report submitted to Indian Institute of Information Technology, Nagpur at DEPARTMENT of COMPUTER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING . The aim of the project is to develop a smart tutoring application that can dynamically change the content of the tutoring sessions and the instructional approach based on feedback provided by the learners .
abstract: The aim of the project of the so-called AI Powered Tutor is to develop a smart tutoring application that can dynamically change the content of the tutoring sessions and the instructional approach based on the feedback provided by the learners . The system has two moderated learning models that classify academic topic and pedagogical mode (Explanation, Knowledge, or Socratic mode) of queries by students with an accuracy of 95% on test accuracy .
